Keywords: tax, Nigeria, FIRS, NRS, law, 2025, NTA

Question_EN: Why do we pay tax in Nigeria?
Answer_EN: We pay tax so the government can provide public services like roads, hospitals, schools, electricity and security for all Nigerians.
Keywords: tax, Nigeria, FIRS, NRS, law, 2025, NTA

Question_EN: What is the purpose of taxation?
Answer_EN: Tax is a compulsory financial contribution imposed by law on individuals and companies to fund government services such as roads, schools, hospitals, and security.
Keywords: tax, Nigeria, FIRS, NRS, law, 2025, NTA

Question_EN: Who collects tax in Nigeria?
Answer_EN: The Federal Inland Revenue Service (FIRS/NRS) collects federal taxes like CIT, VAT, and WHT. State Internal Revenue Services collect Personal Income Tax (PIT) from individuals.
Keywords: tax, Nigeria, FIRS, NRS, law, 2025, NTA

Question_EN: Is tax compulsory in Nigeria?
Answer_EN: Yes, tax is compulsory in Nigeria. The Nigeria Tax Act 2025 makes it a legal obligation for all taxable persons to pay their assessed taxes.
Keywords: tax, Nigeria, FIRS, NRS, law, 2025, NTA

Question_EN: What happens if I don't pay tax?
Answer_EN: Failure to pay tax results in penalties, interest charges, potential legal prosecution, asset seizure, and bank account restrictions under the Nigeria Tax Act 2025.
Keywords: tax, Nigeria, FIRS, NRS, law, 2025, NTA

Question_EN: What are the types of taxes in Nigeria?
Answer_EN: The main taxes in Nigeria are: Personal Income Tax (PIT), Company Income Tax (CIT), Value Added Tax (VAT), Withholding Tax (WHT), Capital Gains Tax (CGT), Stamp Duty, and the Development Levy.
Keywords: tax, Nigeria, FIRS, NRS, law, 2025, NTA

Question_EN: What is the new tax law in Nigeria?
Answer_EN: The Nigeria Tax Act 2025 (signed June 26, 2025) consolidates all tax laws into one. It introduced zero tax for small companies, a 4% Development Levy, rent relief, and digital tax provisions.
Keywords: tax, Nigeria, FIRS, NRS, law, 2025, NTA

Question_EN: Has the tax system changed recently?
Answer_EN: Yes. The Nigeria Tax Act 2025 came into effect on January 1, 2026. It consolidates 10 old tax laws, simplifies rates, protects low-income earners, and introduces new digital tax rules.
Keywords: tax, Nigeria, FIRS, NRS, law, 2025, NTA

Question_EN: What does Nigerian tax law say about individuals?
Answer_EN: Nigerian tax law (NTA 2025) requires all adults to register for TIN, file annual returns, and pay tax on their income using progressive bands from 0% to 25%.
Keywords: tax, Nigeria, FIRS, NRS, law, 2025, NTA

Question_EN: How do I get a TIN in Nigeria?
Answer_EN: Register online at tin.jtb.gov.ng or visit any FIRS/State IRS office. You need your NIN, a valid ID, and email address. It is free and takes 24–48 hours online.
Keywords: TIN, Tax ID, register, FIRS, NIN, JTB, online

Question_EN: What is a Tax Identification Number?
Answer_EN: A TIN (Tax Identification Number) is a unique identifier issued by the Nigeria Revenue Service to every taxable individual and company for tracking tax obligations and payments.
Keywords: TIN, Tax ID, register, FIRS, NIN, JTB, online

Question_EN: Where can I register for TIN?
Answer_EN: Register at tin.jtb.gov.ng (online), any FIRS office nationwide, or your State Internal Revenue Service office. Individuals can also register at CAC for business TIN.
Keywords: TIN, Tax ID, register, FIRS, NIN, JTB, online

Question_EN: Can I register for TIN online?
Answer_EN: Yes, TIN registration is fully available online at tin.jtb.gov.ng. You need your NIN (National Identification Number), email address, and a valid phone number.
Keywords: TIN, Tax ID, register, FIRS, NIN, JTB, online

Question_EN: Do I need TIN as a student?
Answer_EN: All adults over 18 are required to register for TIN. As a student, if you earn any income (business, freelance, allowance), you need a TIN. Even without income, registration is encouraged.
Keywords: TIN, Tax ID, register, FIRS, NIN, JTB, online

Question_EN: Is TIN mandatory for workers?
Answer_EN: Yes. The NTA 2025 makes TIN mandatory for all taxable persons. Banks now require TIN to open accounts, and employers need your TIN to process PAYE deductions.
Keywords: TIN, Tax ID, register, FIRS, NIN, JTB, online

Question_EN: How long does it take to get TIN?
Answer_EN: Online TIN registration takes 24–48 hours. Walk-in registration at FIRS offices can be completed same day in many cases.
Keywords: TIN, Tax ID, register, FIRS, NIN, JTB, online

Question_EN: What documents are needed for TIN registration?
Answer_EN: For individuals: National ID/NIN, passport photograph, utility bill or residence proof, and email address. For companies: CAC certificate, directors' IDs, and company address.
Keywords: TIN, Tax ID, register, FIRS, NIN, JTB, online

Question_EN: Can I have more than one TIN?
Answer_EN: No. Each individual and company can only have one TIN. Having multiple TINs is illegal and can result in penalties under the NTA 2025.
Keywords: TIN, Tax ID, register, FIRS, NIN, JTB, online

Question_EN: How do I recover my lost TIN?
Answer_EN: Visit tin.jtb.gov.ng or any FIRS office with your NIN. Your TIN can be retrieved using your NIN, email, or phone number on your original registration.
Keywords: TIN, Tax ID, register, FIRS, NIN, JTB, online

Question_EN: What is PAYE in Nigeria?
Answer_EN: PAYE (Pay As You Earn) is a system where your employer deducts income tax from your monthly salary before paying you and remits it to your State IRS. It is mandatory for all employees.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: How does PAYE work?
Answer_EN: Your employer calculates your annual tax liability, divides it by 12, deducts the monthly amount from your gross salary, and remits it to your State IRS by the 10th of the following month.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: Who pays PAYE?
Answer_EN: PAYE applies to all employees — salaried workers, contract staff, directors, and others receiving regular employment income. The employer is legally responsible for deducting and remitting.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: Is PAYE deducted automatically?
Answer_EN: Yes. PAYE is automatically deducted from your gross salary by your employer every month before your take-home pay is calculated. You should see it on your payslip.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: How is PAYE calculated?
Answer_EN: PAYE is calculated on your annual gross income minus allowable deductions (pension, NHF, NHIS, life insurance) using progressive tax bands: 0% up to N800k, 15% for N800k-N3m, up to 25% above N50m.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: Can I calculate my PAYE myself?
Answer_EN: Yes. Gross Income minus deductions = Chargeable Income. Apply progressive tax bands from the Fourth Schedule of the NTA 2025. Online PAYE calculators are available on FIRS website.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: What income is subject to PAYE?
Answer_EN: PAYE covers all employment income including basic salary, housing allowance, transport allowance, bonuses, gratuities, and all other cash or non-cash benefits from employment.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: Does PAYE apply to all workers?
Answer_EN: PAYE applies to all employees regardless of contract type (permanent, contract, casual, part-time). Only self-employed individuals and those earning at or below minimum wage are excluded.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: What happens if my employer doesn't deduct PAYE?
Answer_EN: If your employer fails to deduct and remit PAYE, they face a penalty of 10% of the unremitted tax plus CBN interest rate. You as employee may also be assessed directly by the State IRS.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: Can I check my PAYE deductions?
Answer_EN: Yes. Check your monthly payslip for the PAYE deduction line. You can also request a PAYE statement from your employer or verify through your State IRS using your TIN.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: What is Personal Income Tax?
Answer_EN: Personal Income Tax (PIT) is a tax charged on the income of individuals residing in Nigeria. It covers employment income, business profits, investment returns, and other personal income sources.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: Who must pay personal income tax?
Answer_EN: All individuals residing in Nigeria who earn income above the minimum wage threshold must pay PIT. This includes employees, self-employed persons, freelancers, and business owners.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: How much is personal income tax in Nigeria?
Answer_EN: PIT uses progressive rates: 0% on first N800,000; 15% on next N2.2m; 18% on next N9m; 21% on next N13m; 23% on next N25m; 25% on income above N50m annually.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: How is personal income tax calculated?
Answer_EN: Step 1: Add all income. Step 2: Subtract allowable deductions (pension 8%, NHF 2.5%, NHIS, rent relief). Step 3: Apply progressive tax bands from the Fourth Schedule of the NTA 2025.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: What income is taxable?
Answer_EN: Taxable income includes: salary/wages, business profits, rent income, dividends, interest, royalties, capital gains, bonuses, benefits-in-kind, and digital/online income.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: Are allowances taxed?
Answer_EN: Yes. Most employment allowances (housing, transport, meal) are taxable as part of employment income. Only specific items like uniforms and tools provided by employers are excluded.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: Is salary taxable in Nigeria?
Answer_EN: Yes, all salary income is taxable in Nigeria through the PAYE system. Persons earning at or below the National Minimum Wage are exempt from PIT.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: Do freelancers pay income tax?
Answer_EN: Yes. Freelancers pay PIT through Direct Assessment filed with their State IRS. They must register, declare all income annually, and pay by March 31st each year.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: What about self-employed people?
Answer_EN: Self-employed persons are taxed under Direct Assessment. They file annual returns with their State IRS, declare all business income, and pay PIT based on profits minus allowable deductions.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: Do small business owners pay tax?
Answer_EN: Yes. Small business owners pay PIT on business profits (not CIT, which is for registered companies). If profits exceed the minimum wage threshold, tax applies.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: How do I file my tax return?
Answer_EN: File at efiling.firs.gov.ng or your State IRS portal. You need your TIN, income statement, and deduction receipts. Individual deadline is March 31st annually.
Keywords: filing, return, deadline, March 31, e-filing, annual, online

Question_EN: When is the deadline for filing tax?
Answer_EN: Individuals must file by March 31st annually. Companies must file within 6 months after the end of their accounting year. Late filing attracts penalties.
Keywords: filing, return, deadline, March 31, e-filing, annual, online

Question_EN: Can I file tax online in Nigeria?
Answer_EN: Yes. The FIRS e-filing portal (efiling.firs.gov.ng) allows online submission of all tax returns. Most State IRS offices also have online portals for individual PIT filing.
Keywords: filing, return, deadline, March 31, e-filing, annual, online

Question_EN: What happens if I miss the deadline?
Answer_EN: Late filing attracts a penalty of N50,000 for the first month and N25,000 for each subsequent month. Interest also accrues on any unpaid tax at the CBN Monetary Policy Rate.
Keywords: filing, return, deadline, March 31, e-filing, annual, online

Question_EN: How often should I file tax?
Answer_EN: Individuals file once a year by March 31st. Companies file once a year within 6 months of their financial year-end. VAT-registered businesses file monthly VAT returns.
Keywords: filing, return, deadline, March 31, e-filing, annual, online

Question_EN: What documents are required for filing?
Answer_EN: Required: TIN, salary slips or income statements, pension contribution statements, NHF receipts, rent receipts, bank statements, invoices (for business), and prior year tax returns.
Keywords: filing, return, deadline, March 31, e-filing, annual, online

Question_EN: Can someone file tax on my behalf?
Answer_EN: Yes. A tax consultant, accountant, or authorized agent can file on your behalf using a signed authority letter. You remain legally responsible for the accuracy of the return.
Keywords: filing, return, deadline, March 31, e-filing, annual, online

Question_EN: Do I need an accountant to file tax?
Answer_EN: Not mandatory, but advisable for complex returns. Simple PAYE-based returns can be filed yourself online. For businesses, self-employed persons, or multiple income sources, an accountant helps.
Keywords: filing, return, deadline, March 31, e-filing, annual, online

Question_EN: How do I correct a mistake in my filing?
Answer_EN: File an amended return with corrections. Submit the amendment to the relevant tax authority with an explanation. Most errors can be corrected within 6 years without prosecution.
Keywords: filing, return, deadline, March 31, e-filing, annual, online

Question_EN: Can I amend my tax return?
Answer_EN: Yes. You can amend a filed return within 6 years. File an amended return at the same tax authority explaining the correction. Simple errors are usually accepted without penalty.
Keywords: filing, return, deadline, March 31, e-filing, annual, online

Question_EN: What is tax compliance?
Answer_EN: Tax compliance means fulfilling all your legal tax obligations: timely registration, accurate filing, on-time payment of taxes, and maintaining adequate records as required by the NTA 2025.
Keywords: compliance, records, audit, 6 years, FIRS, obligations, TIN

Question_EN: How do I stay compliant with tax laws?
Answer_EN: Stay compliant by: registering for TIN, filing annual returns by deadline, paying taxes on time, keeping records for 6 years, and responding promptly to any FIRS or State IRS notices.
Keywords: compliance, records, audit, 6 years, FIRS, obligations, TIN

Question_EN: What are my obligations as a taxpayer?
Answer_EN: Your obligations include: registering for TIN, filing annual returns, paying assessed taxes on time, deducting and remitting WHT as required, keeping records for 6 years, and cooperating with tax authorities.
Keywords: compliance, records, audit, 6 years, FIRS, obligations, TIN

Question_EN: Do I need to keep records?
Answer_EN: Yes. Nigerian tax law requires all taxpayers to keep adequate records of income and expenses. Failure to keep proper records can result in penalties and adverse tax assessments.
Keywords: compliance, records, audit, 6 years, FIRS, obligations, TIN

Question_EN: How long should I keep tax records?
Answer_EN: Under the NTA 2025, taxpayers must retain all tax-related records, books, and documents for a minimum of 6 years. In cases of fraud, there is no time limit.
Keywords: compliance, records, audit, 6 years, FIRS, obligations, TIN

Question_EN: What happens during a tax audit?
Answer_EN: During an audit, the tax authority reviews your financial records, tax returns, bank statements, and supporting documents to verify accuracy. You have the right to legal representation.
Keywords: compliance, records, audit, 6 years, FIRS, obligations, TIN

Question_EN: Can I be audited anytime?
Answer_EN: Yes. The tax authority can initiate an audit at any time within 6 years of the return filing date. For fraud cases, there is no time limit on audits.
Keywords: compliance, records, audit, 6 years, FIRS, obligations, TIN

Question_EN: What triggers a tax audit?
Answer_EN: Audits are triggered by: significant discrepancy between income declared and lifestyle, tip-offs from third parties, inconsistent returns over years, high-risk industry classification, or random selection.
Keywords: compliance, records, audit, 6 years, FIRS, obligations, TIN

Question_EN: How do I prepare for an audit?
Answer_EN: Prepare by: organizing all financial records for the audit period, reconciling your filed returns with bank statements, engaging a tax professional, and responding promptly and honestly to all queries.
Keywords: compliance, records, audit, 6 years, FIRS, obligations, TIN

Question_EN: What documents are checked in an audit?
Answer_EN: Auditors check: income statements, bank statements, invoices and receipts, payroll records, contracts, asset schedules, VAT returns, WHT certificates, and prior year tax returns.
Keywords: compliance, records, audit, 6 years, FIRS, obligations, TIN

Question_EN: What is the penalty for not paying tax?
Answer_EN: Failure to pay assessed tax attracts a penalty of 10% of the unpaid amount, plus interest at the prevailing CBN rate per annum until the tax is fully paid.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: Are there fines for late filing?
Answer_EN: Yes. Late filing attracts N50,000 for the first month and N25,000 for each additional month. Companies face N50,000 first month and N25,000 per subsequent month.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: What happens if I evade tax?
Answer_EN: Tax evasion is a criminal offence. Penalties include fines up to N10 million, forfeiture of assets, and imprisonment of up to 5 years, plus full payment of all evaded taxes with interest.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: Can I go to jail for tax evasion?
Answer_EN: Yes. Tax evasion carries a potential prison sentence of up to 5 years under the NTA 2025, in addition to payment of all taxes owed, penalties, and interest.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: How much is the penalty for late payment?
Answer_EN: Late payment attracts a penalty of 10% of the unpaid tax, plus interest at the CBN Monetary Policy Rate per annum from the due date until full payment.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: Can penalties be waived?
Answer_EN: Yes. Under the NTA 2025, penalties can be waived in circumstances of genuine hardship, natural disaster, first-time offence, or where the taxpayer makes full voluntary disclosure.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: What is interest on unpaid tax?
Answer_EN: Interest accrues at the CBN Monetary Policy Rate per annum on any unpaid tax from the due date until full settlement. The rate adjusts each time CBN changes its benchmark rate.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: How do I avoid tax penalties?
Answer_EN: Avoid penalties by: filing returns on time, paying assessed tax by due dates, deducting and remitting WHT correctly, keeping accurate records, and responding to all tax authority notices.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: What happens if I ignore tax notices?
Answer_EN: Ignoring tax notices leads to escalating enforcement: additional penalties, bank account restrictions, asset seizure, court proceedings, and ultimately criminal prosecution for persistent defaulters.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: Can I appeal a tax penalty?
Answer_EN: Yes. You can object to a penalty assessment within 30 days of receiving the notice. If objection fails, appeal to the Tax Appeal Tribunal within 30 days of the objection decision.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: What is VAT in Nigeria?
Answer_EN: VAT is a consumption tax of 7.5% charged on the value of taxable goods and services supplied in Nigeria. Suppliers collect it from buyers and remit to FIRS monthly.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: What is the current VAT rate?
Answer_EN: The current VAT rate in Nigeria is 7.5% on all taxable supplies. Basic food, medical products, educational materials, electricity, and exported goods/services are zero-rated at 0%.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: Who pays VAT?
Answer_EN: The final consumer pays VAT. However, VAT-registered businesses collect it from customers (output VAT), deduct what they paid to suppliers (input VAT), and remit the difference to FIRS.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: What goods are exempt from VAT?
Answer_EN: Exempt from VAT: land and buildings, money and securities, government licences, military hardware, baby products, shared road transport, and assistive devices for disabled persons.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: Do small businesses charge VAT?
Answer_EN: Only VAT-registered businesses must charge VAT. Businesses with annual turnover below the VAT threshold (generally N25m) are not required to register for VAT.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: How is VAT calculated?
Answer_EN: VAT = 7.5% x selling price. E.g., if goods cost N100,000, VAT = N7,500; total to customer = N107,500. On imports, VAT is calculated on CIF value plus all import duties.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: When do I remit VAT?
Answer_EN: VAT must be remitted to FIRS by the 21st day of the month following the month in which the supply was made. Monthly filing and payment is mandatory for registered taxpayers.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: What is input VAT?
Answer_EN: Input VAT is the VAT you paid on purchases and expenses made in the course of your business. You deduct it from the output VAT you collected before remitting to FIRS.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: What is output VAT?
Answer_EN: Output VAT is the VAT you charge and collect from your customers on your sales and services. It is the total VAT liability before deducting input VAT credits.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: Can VAT be refunded?
Answer_EN: Yes. If input VAT exceeds output VAT, you are entitled to a refund or can carry the excess forward as a credit. Apply to FIRS with supporting documents; refunds are processed within 90 days.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: Do freelancers pay tax in Nigeria?
Answer_EN: Yes. Freelancers pay PIT through Direct Assessment with their State IRS. All freelance income from any source (local or foreign) must be declared annually by March 31st.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: How do self-employed people pay tax?
Answer_EN: Self-employed persons file under Direct Assessment at their State IRS. Declare all business income, deduct allowable expenses, calculate PIT on net profit, and pay by March 31st annually.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: Do I need to register my business for tax?
Answer_EN: Yes. Any business earning income must be registered for tax. Sole proprietors register with their State IRS for PIT. Companies register with CAC and FIRS for CIT.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: What taxes apply to small businesses?
Answer_EN: Small businesses pay: PIT on profits (if unregistered/sole trader), or 0% CIT (if registered company with turnover < N100m), VAT (if turnover > N25m), and WHT on certain payments received.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: How do I calculate my business tax?
Answer_EN: Business tax = Net profit (revenue minus allowable business expenses) taxed at PIT rates (for sole traders) or 0%/30% CIT (for registered companies). Deduct capital allowances on business assets.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: Do online businesses pay tax?
Answer_EN: Yes. Online businesses are taxable in Nigeria. Income from e-commerce, digital services, social media, freelancing platforms, and app stores is all subject to Nigerian PIT or CIT.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: What if I earn income from abroad?
Answer_EN: Foreign income is taxable in Nigeria if you are a tax resident. However, income brought in through approved banking channels and dividend/rent from abroad are exempt from further Nigerian tax.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: How do I declare business income?
Answer_EN: File your annual PIT return with your State IRS (for sole traders) or CIT return with FIRS (for companies). Attach a profit/loss account showing all income and deductible expenses.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: Do side hustles need tax payment?
Answer_EN: Yes. Income from side hustles is taxable as part of your total annual income. Add all income sources and file one combined return with your State IRS.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: What is presumptive tax?
Answer_EN: Presumptive tax is a simplified tax regime for small businesses and informal sector operators where actual income is hard to determine. Tax is assessed based on business type, location, or other indicators.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: Who is exempt from paying tax?
Answer_EN: Exemptions include: minimum wage earners, pension recipients (approved schemes), military personnel (salaries), government bond holders, disability compensation recipients, and agricultural businesses in first 5 years.
Keywords: exempt, relief, pension, minimum wage, NGO, deduction, N800k
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 162 & Fifth Schedule

Question_EN: Are students exempt from tax?
Answer_EN: Students without income are not required to pay tax. However, all adults (18+) must register for TIN. If a student earns any income, that income is taxable above the minimum wage threshold.
Keywords: exempt, relief, pension, minimum wage, NGO, deduction, N800k
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 162 & Fifth Schedule

Question_EN: Do retirees pay tax?
Answer_EN: Pension income from approved pension schemes under the Pension Reform Act is tax-exempt. However, if a retiree earns additional income (e.g., rental, investment, consultancy), that income is taxable.
Keywords: exempt, relief, pension, minimum wage, NGO, deduction, N800k
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 162 & Fifth Schedule

Question_EN: Are there tax reliefs in Nigeria?
Answer_EN: Yes. Allowable reliefs include: pension contributions (8% of basic salary), NHF contributions (2.5%), NHIS contributions, life insurance premiums, and rent relief of 20% of annual rent capped at N500,000.
Keywords: exempt, relief, pension, minimum wage, NGO, deduction, N800k
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 162 & Fifth Schedule

Question_EN: What is tax relief allowance?
Answer_EN: Tax exemptions in Nigeria include: minimum wage earners (0% PIT), government bonds income, pension income, military salaries, disability compensation, and agricultural businesses for first 5 years.
Keywords: exempt, relief, pension, minimum wage, NGO, deduction, N800k
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 162 & Fifth Schedule

Question_EN: How do I claim tax relief?
Answer_EN: Claim reliefs when filing your annual return by providing supporting evidence: pension statements, NHF receipts, NHIS cards, life insurance certificates, and rent payment receipts.
Keywords: exempt, relief, pension, minimum wage, NGO, deduction, N800k
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 162 & Fifth Schedule

Question_EN: Are NGOs exempt from tax?
Answer_EN: NGOs and charities are exempt on income from non-commercial activities. Any income from trade or business carried on by an NGO is taxable. They must file annual returns regardless.
Keywords: exempt, relief, pension, minimum wage, NGO, deduction, N800k
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 162 & Fifth Schedule

Question_EN: What income is tax-free?
Answer_EN: Tax-free income includes: minimum wage salaries, pension (approved scheme), government bond interest, disability pensions, military salaries, agricultural income (first 5 years), and foreign dividends/rent via approved channels.
Keywords: exempt, relief, pension, minimum wage, NGO, deduction, N800k
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 162 & Fifth Schedule

Question_EN: Do disabled persons get tax relief?
Answer_EN: Yes. Compensation for disability/personal injury up to N50 million is exempt from tax. Assistive devices are also VAT-exempt. Specific disability reliefs can be claimed through the annual return.
Keywords: exempt, relief, pension, minimum wage, NGO, deduction, N800k
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 162 & Fifth Schedule

Question_EN: Are gifts taxable?
Answer_EN: Gifts of assets are generally not taxable in the hands of the recipient. However, the giver may be subject to CGT if there is a gain on disposal. Regular gifts forming part of income may be assessed.
Keywords: exempt, relief, pension, minimum wage, NGO, deduction, N800k
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 162 & Fifth Schedule

Question_EN: Is there tax on digital services?
Answer_EN: Yes. The NTA 2025 introduced comprehensive digital tax rules. Income from digital services, online platforms, e-commerce, social media, and streaming is subject to Nigerian tax.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: Do online platforms pay tax in Nigeria?
Answer_EN: Foreign online platforms with significant economic presence in Nigeria (streaming, app stores, digital ads, e-commerce) must register with FIRS and pay tax on income derived from Nigerian users.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: Is social media income taxable?
Answer_EN: Yes. All income earned through social media (sponsored posts, affiliate marketing, brand deals, content creation) is taxable as part of your total annual income.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: Do content creators pay tax?
Answer_EN: Yes. Income from YouTube, TikTok, Instagram, podcasting, blogging, and other content creation platforms is taxable as self-employment income under PIT in Nigeria.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: What about crypto income tax?
Answer_EN: Cryptocurrency gains are taxable in Nigeria as chargeable assets under the NTA 2025. Profits from buying and selling crypto are subject to Capital Gains Tax (CGT) at progressive individual rates.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: Are foreign digital companies taxed?
Answer_EN: Yes. Foreign digital companies (Netflix, Google, Meta, Amazon) with significant economic presence in Nigeria are taxed on income attributable to Nigerian users under the NTA 2025.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: What is digital tax policy?
Answer_EN: Nigeria's digital tax policy under NTA 2025 uses Significant Economic Presence (SEP) rules to tax foreign digital companies. It also taxes all domestic digital income including crypto, social media, and remote work.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: Is YouTube income taxable?
Answer_EN: Yes. YouTube AdSense income is taxable as self-employment income in Nigeria. Whether paid in USD or NGN, it must be declared and converted to Naira at the CBN rate for tax purposes.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: Do influencers pay tax?
Answer_EN: Yes. Influencers and content creators are classified as self-employed individuals. All brand deals, sponsored content, affiliate income, and AdSense must be declared and taxed annually.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: How do I report online earnings?
Answer_EN: Declare all online income in your annual PIT return under 'other income' or 'self-employment income'. Convert foreign currency amounts to Naira at CBN rate on the transaction date.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: wetin be PAYE?
Answer_EN: PAYE means your oga (employer) go remove your tax from salary every month before you collect am, then send the money go State tax board. You no need do anything yourself.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: PAYE dey work how?
Answer_EN: Every month, your company calculate how much tax you suppose pay for the year, divide am by 12, cut am from your salary, then send am go State tax office.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: na who dey pay PAYE?
Answer_EN: Every person wey dey collect salary — permanent, contract, or part-time — PAYE concern them. Your employer dey handle the deduction and payment.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: dem dey remove PAYE direct?
Answer_EN: Yes, your employer dey remove am direct from your salary every month. Check your payslip — you go see 'Tax' or 'PAYE' deduction line.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: how dem dey calculate PAYE?
Answer_EN: Dem go add your total yearly salary, minus your pension and NHF contributions, then apply tax bands: first N800k — 0%, next N2.2m — 15%, and so on up to 25% for high earners.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: I fit calculate PAYE by myself?
Answer_EN: Yes, you fit. Take your annual salary, minus pension (8%), NHF (2.5%), NHIS, and rent relief. Then apply tax band: first N800k at 0%, next N2.2m at 15%, etc.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: which money PAYE dey touch?
Answer_EN: PAYE dey touch your base salary, housing allowance, transport, bonus, and any benefit your employer give you. All na employment income.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: all workers dey pay PAYE?
Answer_EN: All workers dey pay PAYE except those earning minimum wage or below. Self-employed people pay through Direct Assessment instead of PAYE.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: if my oga no remove PAYE nko?
Answer_EN: Your oga go face penalty — 10% of the tax plus interest. And your State tax board fit come after you directly for the tax you never pay.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: how I go see my PAYE record?
Answer_EN: Check your payslip every month. You can also go your State tax board with your TIN to see your PAYE payment history. Your employer must give you annual PAYE certificate.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: wetin be personal income tax?

Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: na who must pay tax?
Answer_EN: Any person wey dey live and earn money for Nigeria suppose pay PIT. Na from salary, business, rent, investment — any income source above minimum wage.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive

Question_EN: tax dey how much?
Answer_EN: First N800k: free (0%). N800k–N3m: 15%. N3m–N12m: 18%. N12m–N25m: 21%. N25m–N50m: 23%. Above N50m: 25%. Most ordinary Nigerians fall in the lower bands.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: how dem dey calculate income tax?
Answer_EN: Add your total income, minus pension, NHF, and NHIS contributions, minus rent relief. Then use the tax bands: first N800k free, next N2.2m at 15%, and so on.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: which income dem dey tax?
Answer_EN: All income sources dey taxed: salary, business profit, rent, investment income, online money, crypto gains, freelance income. But pension, government bonds, and military salary get exemption.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: allowance sef dey taxed?
Answer_EN: Yes, your housing allowance, transport allowance, and meal allowance na all part of your taxable income. Your employer include them when calculating your PAYE.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: salary dey taxed?

Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Answer_EN: Yes, freelancer suppose pay tax. Go your State tax board, register for Direct Assessment, declare your income every year, and pay by March 31.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive

Question_EN: wetin be VAT?
Answer_EN: VAT na 7.5% tax wey dem dey add on top of most goods and services. The seller dey collect am from buyer, then send am go FIRS every month.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: VAT rate na how much?
Answer_EN: 7.5% na the standard VAT rate. But food, medicine, school materials, electricity, and exported goods na 0% (zero-rated). Some items like land and government licences are exempt.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: na who dey pay VAT?
Answer_EN: The person wey buy the goods or service dey pay VAT. But the business collect am, minus their own input VAT, then send the balance go FIRS monthly.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: which goods no dey VAT?
Answer_EN: Land, buildings, money, government licence, baby products, danfo/keke transport, military equipment, and disability aids no get VAT. Basic food, medicine, electricity na 0% (zero-rated).
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: small biz dey charge VAT?
Answer_EN: If your turnover dey below N25m, you no need register for VAT and no need collect am. But if you register voluntarily, you must follow all VAT rules.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: how dem dey calculate VAT?
Answer_EN: VAT = 7.5% of the price. If something cost N10,000, VAT na N750, total na N10,750. That N750 na what the seller go remit go FIRS.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: when I go pay VAT?
Answer_EN: Remit every month — before 21st of next month. So January VAT must reach FIRS by 21st February. Late remittance get penalty.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: wetin be input VAT?
Answer_EN: Input VAT na the VAT wey you pay when you buy things for your business. You fit use am reduce the VAT you owe FIRS. E like refund on your business purchases.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: wetin be output VAT?
Answer_EN: Output VAT na the VAT you dey collect from your customers when you sell goods or service. The difference between output VAT and input VAT na what you send FIRS.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: VAT fit return?
Answer_EN: Yes. If you pay more input VAT than you collect as output VAT, you can apply to FIRS for refund or carry-forward credit. Process takes up to 90 days.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: freelancer dey pay tax?

Question_EN: Is income tax deducted automatically?
Answer_EN: Yes. Through the PAYE system, your employer automatically deducts income tax from your gross salary every month before remitting to your State IRS. No action is required from you.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: Do employers handle tax payments?
Answer_EN: Yes. Employers are legally responsible for calculating, deducting, and remitting PAYE on behalf of their employees to the relevant State IRS by the 10th of each following month.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: Why is tax removed from salary?
Answer_EN: PAYE (Pay As You Earn) is a tax deduction system where employers deduct income tax from employees' salaries monthly and remit it directly to the relevant State Internal Revenue Service.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: Can I opt out of PAYE?
Answer_EN: No. PAYE is mandatory for all employees. It cannot be opted out of. Only truly self-employed individuals without an employer pay tax differently through Direct Assessment.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: How do I verify salary deductions?
Answer_EN: Request a detailed payslip from HR, compare PAYE deductions against online calculators, or visit your State IRS with your TIN to verify that your PAYE is being remitted correctly.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: Is PAYE the same globally?

Question_EN: Do all jobs have PAYE?

Question_EN: What happens if PAYE is incorrect?

Question_EN: Can I get refund on PAYE?

Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit

Question_EN: Who ensures PAYE is correct?

Question_EN: What is taxable income?
Answer_EN: Taxable income is your total income from all sources minus allowable deductions (pension, NHF, NHIS, rent relief, and life insurance premiums). Tax applies only to this net figure.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: How much tax should I pay?
Answer_EN: Calculate: Total income minus allowable deductions = Chargeable income. Apply progressive rates: 0% first N800k, 15% next N2.2m, 18% next N9m, 21% next N13m, 23% next N25m, 25% above N50m.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: Do part-time workers pay tax?
Answer_EN: Yes. Part-time workers earning above the minimum wage threshold are liable to PIT. Their income is taxed at the same progressive rates as full-time employees.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: Is overtime taxed?
Answer_EN: Yes. Overtime pay is part of employment income and is taxable under PAYE. It is added to your regular salary in the month it is paid before tax is calculated.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: Are bonuses taxed?
Answer_EN: Yes. Bonuses and incentives are part of employment income and are subject to PAYE deduction in the month they are paid, at your applicable marginal tax rate.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: What income is not taxed?
Answer_EN: Tax-free income includes: minimum wage earnings, pension (approved scheme), military salary, government bond interest, disability compensation (up to N50m), and dividends from government companies.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: Do I pay tax on gifts?
Answer_EN: Generally, gifts received are not taxable in the hands of the recipient. However, if gifts form part of a business arrangement or are regular, they may be assessed as income.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: Is pension taxed?
Answer_EN: Pension income and contributions to an approved pension scheme under the Pension Reform Act are tax-exempt. However, any additional investment income earned may be taxable.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: Are savings taxed?
Answer_EN: Interest earned on savings is subject to Withholding Tax (WHT) at 10%, deducted by the bank. Below a certain threshold, it may be the final tax on that income.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: How do I calculate my tax?
Answer_EN: 1. Sum all income. 2. Deduct pension (8% of basic), NHF (2.5%), NHIS, and rent relief (up to N500k). 3. Apply progressive bands: 0% first N800k, 15% next N2.2m, to 25% above N50m.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: What is tax audit process?

Question_EN: Can small businesses be audited?

Question_EN: Why do audits happen?

Question_EN: What should I do during audit?

Question_EN: How do I respond to audit notice?
Answer_EN: Acknowledge receipt within the stated deadline, engage a tax professional, gather all requested documents, and prepare a detailed response addressing each query raised in the audit notice.

Question_EN: What happens if I delay tax payment?

Question_EN: Are there penalties for errors?
Answer_EN: Errors through negligence attract penalties and interest. However, honest errors corrected promptly through amended returns are generally treated more leniently than deliberate non-compliance.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: How much is late tax penalty?
Answer_EN: Late filing: N50,000 (first month) + N25,000 (each additional month). Late payment: 10% of unpaid tax + CBN interest rate per annum from the due date.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: Can penalties be reduced?

Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: Is tax evasion illegal?

Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: What are consequences of tax fraud?

Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: Can I negotiate penalties?

Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: What if I can't pay tax?
Answer_EN: Penalties under the NTA 2025 include: N50,000 for the first month of late filing, N25,000 per subsequent month, 10% penalty for failure to deduct WHT, and imprisonment for tax evasion.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: Do penalties increase over time?

Question_EN: Can I appeal tax decisions?

Question_EN: What is sales tax?
Answer_EN: Nigeria uses VAT (Value Added Tax) rather than a traditional sales tax. VAT of 7.5% is charged at every stage of the supply chain, with businesses recovering input VAT paid to suppliers.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: Is VAT same as sales tax?
Answer_EN: They are similar but different. VAT is charged at every production stage with input tax recovery; sales tax is usually charged only at final sale. Nigeria uses VAT at 7.5%.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: Who charges VAT?
Answer_EN: VAT-registered businesses (those with annual turnover above N25m or voluntary registrants) must charge VAT on their taxable supplies to customers.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: Are services taxed?
Answer_EN: Yes. Most services provided in Nigeria are subject to 7.5% VAT. Exemptions apply to medical services, educational tuition, financial services (on money), and shared passenger transport.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: What items are VAT free?
Answer_EN: VAT-free (exempt): land, buildings, money, government licences, military equipment, baby products, shared road transport. Zero-rated (0% VAT): food, medicine, education, electricity, exports.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: Do online purchases include VAT?
Answer_EN: Online purchases of taxable goods and services attract 7.5% VAT. Foreign digital services sold to Nigerian customers by non-resident suppliers are also subject to VAT under the NTA 2025.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: How is VAT added?
Answer_EN: VAT is calculated as 7.5% of the taxable value of the supply and must be shown separately on your VAT invoice. E.g., goods worth N10,000 + N750 VAT = N10,750 total.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: When is VAT paid?
Answer_EN: VAT is paid by the consumer at the point of purchase. VAT-registered businesses collect and remit it to FIRS by the 21st of the following month.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: Do I need to register for VAT?
Answer_EN: Mandatory if turnover exceeds N25m annually. Voluntary registration is allowed below this threshold. Registration is done at any FIRS office or through the FIRS online portal.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: Can businesses reclaim VAT?
Answer_EN: Yes. VAT-registered businesses can claim input VAT credits for VAT paid on business-related purchases. The net VAT payable to FIRS = Output VAT collected minus Input VAT paid.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: Do freelancers need to pay taxes?
Answer_EN: Yes. Freelancers are self-employed individuals subject to PIT. Register for Direct Assessment with your State IRS, declare all income annually, and pay by March 31st.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: How do I report freelance income?
Answer_EN: Include all freelance income in your annual PIT return. If paid in foreign currency, convert to Naira at the CBN rate on the date received. Deduct allowable business expenses before calculating tax.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: Is gig work taxable?
Answer_EN: Yes. All gig economy income (ride-hailing, delivery, freelance platforms, task apps) is taxable as self-employment income. Declare all earnings in your annual return.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: Do small earnings require tax?
Answer_EN: If total annual income from all sources exceeds the minimum wage threshold (first N800k is taxed at 0%), tax applies to the excess. Even small earnings should be declared.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: What tax applies to startups?
Answer_EN: Registered startups (companies) with turnover below N100m pay 0% CIT. Labelled startups under the Nigeria Startup Act may qualify for additional incentives. All must file returns regardless.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: How do I track my income?
Answer_EN: Keep a simple income record: date, client/source, description, and amount. Use accounting software (Wave, QuickBooks) or a spreadsheet. Reconcile monthly with your bank statement.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: Do I need tax agent?
Answer_EN: Not mandatory but recommended for complex situations. A registered tax consultant helps ensure accurate filing, maximizes legitimate deductions, and handles audit queries professionally.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: Is business income taxed differently?
Answer_EN: For sole traders: business income is taxed as PIT (progressive rates). For registered companies: CIT applies at 0% (small) or 30% (large). The tax base and treatment differ significantly.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: Do I pay tax on side income?
Answer_EN: Yes. All income from all sources is taxable. Side income from part-time work, freelancing, or business is added to your primary income and taxed at your applicable marginal rate.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: How do I declare earnings?

Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: Who qualifies for tax exemption?
Answer_EN: Tax exemptions in Nigeria include: minimum wage earners (0% PIT), government bonds income, pension income, military salaries, disability compensation, and agricultural businesses for first 5 years.
Keywords: exempt, relief, pension, minimum wage, NGO, deduction, N800k
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 162 & Fifth Schedule

Question_EN: Are students exempt globally?
