Question_EN: Is online income taxable worldwide?
Answer_EN: Online income taxation varies by country. In Nigeria, all income earned by tax residents (including online income from foreign platforms) is taxable regardless of where it is earned.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: Do remote workers pay tax?
Answer_EN: Yes. Nigerian tax residents working remotely for foreign companies pay Nigerian PIT on their salary. Foreign employers of Nigerian remote workers do not need to register in Nigeria solely for this.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: How is crypto taxed?
Answer_EN: Crypto gains are taxable at progressive PIT rates for individuals. Each disposal (sale, exchange, or use) may trigger a taxable gain. Mining income is treated as business income.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: Do I report foreign income?
Answer_EN: Yes. Nigerian tax residents must declare all foreign income in their annual return. Income from foreign freelancing, investments, and employment is taxable in Nigeria subject to applicable exemptions.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: Is e-commerce taxed?
Answer_EN: Yes. E-commerce businesses are taxable. Sole trader e-commerce pays PIT; incorporated e-commerce companies pay CIT. VAT at 7.5% applies to taxable goods and services sold online.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: Do digital services have tax?
Answer_EN: Yes. Most digital services (streaming, cloud services, software, digital downloads, online advertising) attract 7.5% VAT. Income from providing these services is also taxable as business income.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: How are apps taxed?
Answer_EN: App income (from app stores, in-app purchases, subscriptions) is taxable as business income. Nigerian developers pay PIT or CIT on profits. Foreign app platforms are taxed on Nigerian revenue.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: Do I pay tax on PayPal income?
Answer_EN: Yes. PayPal income is taxable as it represents business or freelance earnings. Convert to Naira at the CBN rate on the date received and declare in your annual PIT return.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: Is freelancing abroad taxed?
Answer_EN: Yes. If you are a Nigerian tax resident, your freelancing income from abroad is taxable in Nigeria. It must be declared and taxed at your applicable PIT rate after allowable deductions.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: How do I declare digital income?
Answer_EN: Include all digital income (AdSense, brand deals, crypto gains, PayPal earnings) in your annual PIT return. Use the 'other income' or 'self-employment income' section. Convert FX to Naira at CBN rate.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: Do I personally need to pay tax?
Answer_EN: Yes, if you earn income in Nigeria above the minimum wage threshold. Employees pay through PAYE; self-employed persons pay through Direct Assessment. Minimum wage earners are exempt.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: Am I supposed to pay tax with my income?
Answer_EN: Yes. All income-earning Nigerian residents are required to pay PIT. The amount depends on your total annual income. Those earning minimum wage or below pay zero tax.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: Do I owe any tax right now?
Answer_EN: If you are employed, your employer handles PAYE deductions. If self-employed, check if you have filed and paid your annual return. Visit your State IRS with your TIN to check your status.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: How much tax should I pay from my salary?
Answer_EN: Use progressive bands: 0% on first N800k, 15% on N800k-N3m, 18% on N3m-N12m, etc. Subtract pension and NHF contributions first. Monthly PAYE = annual tax divided by 12.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: Is my salary too small to be taxed?
Answer_EN: Persons earning at or below the National Minimum Wage are exempt from PIT under the NTA 2025. If you earn just slightly above, only the amount above the threshold attracts tax.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: Will they tax my allowance too?
Answer_EN: Yes. Most employment allowances (housing, transport, meal) are included in taxable income. Only specific non-cash benefits like uniforms and tools may be excluded.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: Do I pay tax if I earn little money?
Answer_EN: If your total annual income is N800,000 or less, you pay 0% tax. Persons earning the National Minimum Wage or below are completely exempt from PIT.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: Is my side income taxable?
Answer_EN: Yes. All income from all sources must be declared and is taxable. This includes side businesses, freelance work, rental income, and online earnings.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: Do I have to declare all my income?
Answer_EN: Yes. Under the NTA 2025, all taxable persons must declare total income from all sources in their annual return. Non-disclosure is tax evasion, a criminal offence.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: What part of my income is taxable?
Answer_EN: Your chargeable income = Gross Income minus statutory deductions (pension 8%, NHF 2.5%, NHIS, rent relief N500k max). Tax applies only to the chargeable income amount.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: Why is tax removed from my salary?
Answer_EN: Tax is deducted from your salary through PAYE to make tax collection easier and ensure the government receives revenue throughout the year rather than in one lump sum.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: Why is my salary smaller after deductions?
Answer_EN: Your take-home is smaller because of statutory deductions: PAYE (income tax), Pension (8%), NHF (2.5%), NHIS, and any company-specific deductions. All these are required by law.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: How do I know if my PAYE is correct?
Answer_EN: Check your monthly payslip for the tax deduction. Compare it against your annual salary using the FIRS PAYE calculator or the Fourth Schedule tax bands in the NTA 2025.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: Can I stop my employer from deducting tax?
Answer_EN: No. PAYE deduction is a legal obligation. Your employer has no choice but to deduct and remit. Requesting them to stop is asking them to break the law.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: Is my employer cheating me on tax?
Answer_EN: Compare your payslip deduction to the tax calculated on your gross income using the NTA 2025 bands. If the figures differ significantly, contact your State IRS or a tax professional for advice.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: How much PAYE should be deducted from me?
Answer_EN: Use progressive bands: 0% on first N800k annually, 15% on next N2.2m, 18% on next N9m, 21% on next N13m, 23% on next N25m, 25% above N50m. Monthly PAYE = annual tax / 12.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: Can I get refund from excess PAYE?
Answer_EN: Yes. If excess PAYE was deducted, apply for a refund from your State IRS within 6 years with evidence of your tax payments and a completed refund application form.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: Why is my tax so high this month?
Answer_EN: Your tax may be higher if you received a bonus, commission, or lump-sum payment this month. These are added to your monthly salary before tax is calculated.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: Can I see breakdown of my tax?
Answer_EN: Yes. Your employer must provide a detailed payslip showing all deductions. You can also request a Deduction of Tax at Source (DTS) certificate from your employer annually.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: Who do I complain to about PAYE?
Answer_EN: Complain to your State Internal Revenue Service (e.g., KIRS, LIRS, BIRS). If no resolution, escalate to the Tax Ombudsman established under the NTA 2025.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: Do I need a TIN before I start work?
Answer_EN: Yes. Your employer needs your TIN to process PAYE. Register before your first payslip to ensure your tax deductions are properly credited to your account.
Keywords: TIN, Tax ID, register, FIRS, NIN, JTB, online

Question_EN: Can I work without a TIN?
Answer_EN: Technically you can work, but your employer cannot properly remit your PAYE without a TIN. You also risk penalties and inability to access government services. Register immediately.
Keywords: TIN, Tax ID, register, FIRS, NIN, JTB, online

Question_EN: How do I register my own TIN?
Answer_EN: A Tax Identification Number (TIN) is a unique number assigned to every taxpayer by the Nigeria Revenue Service (NRS) for tax identification and compliance purposes.
Keywords: TIN, Tax ID, register, FIRS, NIN, JTB, online

Question_EN: Is TIN registration stressful?
Answer_EN: No longer. The JTB online portal (tin.jtb.gov.ng) has simplified TIN registration significantly. Most people complete it in under 10 minutes with just their NIN.
Keywords: TIN, Tax ID, register, FIRS, NIN, JTB, online

Question_EN: Can someone do TIN for me?
Answer_EN: Yes. An authorized agent (accountant, tax consultant) can register TIN on your behalf with a signed authority letter. Ensure the agent is legitimate to protect your personal information.
Keywords: TIN, Tax ID, register, FIRS, NIN, JTB, online

Question_EN: Do I need to go to office for TIN?
Answer_EN: No. TIN registration is fully available online at tin.jtb.gov.ng. Office visits are only needed for complex cases or corrections that require document verification.
Keywords: TIN, Tax ID, register, FIRS, NIN, JTB, online

Question_EN: What if I never registered for TIN?
Answer_EN: Register now immediately. There may be a penalty for late registration, but proactive compliance reduces your exposure. Visit tin.jtb.gov.ng or your nearest State IRS office.
Keywords: TIN, Tax ID, register, FIRS, NIN, JTB, online

Question_EN: Will they penalize me for no TIN?
Answer_EN: Yes. Under the NTA 2025, failure to register for a TIN attracts a penalty of N50,000 for the first month and N25,000 for each subsequent month of default.
Keywords: TIN, Tax ID, register, FIRS, NIN, JTB, online

Question_EN: Can I use one TIN for two jobs?
Answer_EN: Yes. Your single TIN covers all your income sources. Your employers use the same TIN to remit PAYE to your State IRS.
Keywords: TIN, Tax ID, register, FIRS, NIN, JTB, online

Question_EN: How do I confirm my TIN is valid?
Answer_EN: Verify your TIN at tin.jtb.gov.ng or call the FIRS helpline (0700-CALL-FIRS). You can also verify at any FIRS office or State tax office.
Keywords: TIN, Tax ID, register, FIRS, NIN, JTB, online

Question_EN: Do I have to file tax myself?
Answer_EN: If employed, your employer handles PAYE but you still need to file a self-assessment return annually. If self-employed, you must file your own return by March 31st each year.
Keywords: filing, return, deadline, March 31, e-filing, annual, online

Question_EN: What happens if I don't file my tax?
Answer_EN: Non-filing leads to penalties of N50,000 (first month) + N25,000 (each additional month), interest charges, possible tax assessment by the tax authority, and eventually court action.
Keywords: filing, return, deadline, March 31, e-filing, annual, online

Question_EN: Can I ignore tax filing?
Answer_EN: No. Ignoring tax filing results in accumulating penalties, possible audit, tax assessment, and ultimately enforcement action including asset seizure and prosecution.
Keywords: filing, return, deadline, March 31, e-filing, annual, online

Question_EN: How do I start filing my tax?
Answer_EN: Step 1: Gather all income documents. Step 2: Register/login at efiling.firs.gov.ng with your TIN. Step 3: Complete the return form. Step 4: Upload documents. Step 5: Submit and pay any assessed tax.
Keywords: filing, return, deadline, March 31, e-filing, annual, online

Question_EN: Can I file my tax late?
Answer_EN: Yes, but penalties apply. N50,000 for the first month late and N25,000 for each subsequent month. File as soon as possible even if you cannot pay immediately to stop penalties accumulating.
Keywords: filing, return, deadline, March 31, e-filing, annual, online

Question_EN: Will I be fined for not filing?
Answer_EN: Yes. The penalty for not filing is N50,000 for the first month and N25,000 for every month thereafter. The fine accumulates until you file. File immediately to stop the penalty.
Keywords: filing, return, deadline, March 31, e-filing, annual, online

Question_EN: Can I correct my tax after submitting?
Answer_EN: Yes. File an amended return within 6 years of the original filing. The amendment must be submitted to the same tax office with a letter explaining the correction.
Keywords: filing, return, deadline, March 31, e-filing, annual, online

Question_EN: What if I made mistake in my filing?
Answer_EN: Tax returns must be filed annually. Individuals file by March 31st; companies file within 6 months after their financial year end. Returns can be filed online through the FIRS e-filing portal at efiling.firs.gov.ng.
Keywords: filing, return, deadline, March 31, e-filing, annual, online

Question_EN: Do I need help to file tax?
Answer_EN: For simple salary income, you can file yourself online. For complex situations (multiple income, business, investments), a qualified tax professional ensures accuracy and optimal deductions.
Keywords: filing, return, deadline, March 31, e-filing, annual, online

Question_EN: Can I file tax without documents?
Answer_EN: You should not. Without documents, your figures will be estimates and may be challenged in an audit. Gather payslips, bank statements, and receipts before filing. Estimates can lead to under/over-payment.
Keywords: filing, return, deadline, March 31, e-filing, annual, online

Question_EN: Am I compliant with tax laws?
Answer_EN: Check if you have: registered for TIN, filed all annual returns, paid all assessed taxes, properly deducted and remitted WHT, and maintained records for 6 years. Any gap means non-compliance.
Keywords: compliance, records, audit, 6 years, FIRS, obligations, TIN

Question_EN: How do I know if I am following tax rules?
Answer_EN: You are compliant if: your TIN is active, all returns are filed by deadline, all assessed taxes are paid, WHT is deducted where required, and your records are up to date.
Keywords: compliance, records, audit, 6 years, FIRS, obligations, TIN

Question_EN: What if I have not been paying tax?
Answer_EN: Voluntary disclosure is your best option. Approach your State IRS or FIRS, declare your outstanding liabilities, and negotiate a payment plan. Penalties may be reduced for genuine voluntary compliance.
Keywords: compliance, records, audit, 6 years, FIRS, obligations, TIN

Question_EN: Will they audit me personally?
Answer_EN: You can be audited if you are self-employed, have multiple income sources, show lifestyle inconsistent with declared income, or are selected through the tax authority's risk-based audit process.
Keywords: compliance, records, audit, 6 years, FIRS, obligations, TIN

Question_EN: Can I get into trouble for mistakes?
Answer_EN: Honest mistakes are generally not criminally prosecuted but may attract additional tax and interest. However, willful falsification, tax evasion, or fraud is a criminal offence with fines and imprisonment.
Keywords: compliance, records, audit, 6 years, FIRS, obligations, TIN

Question_EN: How do I prepare for tax audit?
Answer_EN: Organize all financial documents for the audit period, reconcile returns with bank statements, note all deductions claimed with supporting evidence, and engage a tax professional to guide you through the process.
Keywords: compliance, records, audit, 6 years, FIRS, obligations, TIN

Question_EN: Do I need to keep all my receipts?
Answer_EN: Yes. Keep all receipts, invoices, bank statements, and payment records. These are your primary evidence for deductions claimed and income declared in your tax returns.
Keywords: compliance, records, audit, 6 years, FIRS, obligations, TIN

Question_EN: What if I lost my tax records?
Answer_EN: Try to reconstruct records from bank statements, employer certificates, and counterparties. Inform your tax authority proactively. Inability to provide records during an audit leads to adverse assessments.
Keywords: compliance, records, audit, 6 years, FIRS, obligations, TIN

Question_EN: Can they check my bank account?
Answer_EN: Yes. Under the NTA 2025, the tax authority can obtain a court order to access your bank records. Banks are also required to report certain transactions to the NRS.
Keywords: compliance, records, audit, 6 years, FIRS, obligations, TIN

Question_EN: How serious is tax compliance?
Answer_EN: Very serious. Non-compliance leads to: financial penalties, interest charges, reputational damage, inability to obtain TCC (needed for contracts), asset seizure, and criminal prosecution for persistent defaulters.
Keywords: compliance, records, audit, 6 years, FIRS, obligations, TIN

Question_EN: Will I be punished for not paying tax?
Answer_EN: Yes. Failure to pay results in penalties (10% of unpaid tax), interest charges, potential bank account restrictions, asset seizure, and ultimately criminal prosecution.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: What happens if I skip tax payment?
Answer_EN: Skipping tax payment results in: 10% penalty on unpaid tax, CBN interest rate charges, bank account restrictions, eventual asset seizure, and court proceedings if persistent.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: Can I go to jail for my tax issues?
Answer_EN: Criminal prosecution and imprisonment are possible for deliberate tax evasion, providing false information, or fraud. Honest mistakes or inability to pay are handled differently through civil enforcement.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: How much will I be fined?
Answer_EN: Late filing: N50,000 (first month) + N25,000 (per month). Unpaid tax: 10% penalty + CBN interest. Tax evasion/fraud: up to N10 million fine + possible imprisonment.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: Can I settle my tax penalty?
Answer_EN: Yes. Contact your tax authority to discuss settlement options. You can pay penalties in full, apply for a penalty waiver, or negotiate a payment plan for the total outstanding amount.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: Will penalty keep increasing?
Answer_EN: Yes. Penalties and interest accumulate daily/monthly until the full amount is paid. The longer you wait, the larger the total amount owed becomes.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: Can I beg them to reduce penalty?
Answer_EN: Yes, formally. File a penalty waiver application with supporting evidence (hardship, medical emergency, natural disaster, etc.). The tax authority has discretion to reduce or cancel penalties.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: What if I can't afford to pay tax?
Answer_EN: Contact the tax authority immediately to apply for a payment plan (instalment agreement). The NTA 2025 allows for structured payment arrangements to settle tax debts over time.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: Will they block my account?
Answer_EN: Yes. Persistent non-payment can lead to bank account restriction orders under the NTA 2025. The tax authority can instruct your bank to restrict withdrawals until outstanding taxes are settled.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: Can I ignore tax messages?
Answer_EN: No. Ignoring tax messages results in escalating enforcement actions. Respond to every notice within the stated deadline. Even if you cannot pay, communicate with the tax authority.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: Do I need to pay tax as a freelancer?
Answer_EN: Yes. As a self-employed person, you pay PIT through Direct Assessment at your State IRS. Register, track your income and expenses, file by March 31st, and pay the assessed tax.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: I work for myself do I still pay tax?
Answer_EN: Yes. Self-employed individuals are not exempt from tax. Register for Direct Assessment at your State IRS, declare your income and expenses annually, and pay tax on your net profit.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: How do I calculate my own tax?
Answer_EN: Calculate: Total income minus allowable expenses (rent, supplies, transport) minus statutory deductions (pension, NHF). Apply progressive PIT rates to the resulting chargeable income.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: Do I pay tax on small business?
Answer_EN: Yes, if profits exceed the minimum wage threshold. Registered companies with turnover below N100m pay 0% CIT. Sole traders pay PIT on net business profits regardless of business size.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: My business is small do I still pay tax?
Answer_EN: Yes. If you are a sole trader, you pay PIT on profits. If incorporated, 0% CIT applies if turnover is below N100m. But all businesses must file returns and register for TIN.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: What if my business is not registered?
Answer_EN: Unregistered businesses can still be assessed for tax. You also miss out on tax incentives, VAT credits, and the credibility of a registered business. Register with CAC as soon as possible.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: Can I hide some income?
Answer_EN: No. Deliberately hiding income is tax evasion, a criminal offence under the NTA 2025. Penalties include fines up to N10m and imprisonment. All income from all sources must be declared.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: How do I declare my earnings?
Answer_EN: Include all earnings (salary, business, freelance, investment, foreign) in your annual tax return. Use your State IRS portal or FIRS e-filing portal. Keep supporting documents for verification.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: Do I pay tax on cash payments?
Answer_EN: Yes. Cash income is still income. All receipts regardless of payment method (cash, transfer, crypto, barter) must be declared. The method of payment does not determine taxability.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: Do I need to charge VAT for my business?
Answer_EN: If your annual turnover exceeds N25 million, you must register for VAT and charge 7.5% on all taxable supplies. Below this threshold, registration is voluntary.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: My business is small should I charge VAT?
Answer_EN: If turnover is below N25m, you are not required to charge VAT. However, voluntary registration is allowed and may benefit businesses claiming input VAT credits on purchases.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: What happens if I don't charge VAT?
Answer_EN: Failure to charge and remit VAT when required attracts a penalty of 10% of the unremitted VAT plus CBN interest. Persistent non-compliance can lead to prosecution.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: Do I include VAT in my price?
Answer_EN: VAT must be shown separately on your VAT invoice. Your selling price can be VAT-inclusive (you absorb it) or VAT-exclusive (added on top). Most businesses add VAT on top.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: How do I calculate VAT for my sales?
Answer_EN: Multiply your sale price by 7.5%. E.g., N50,000 sale: VAT = N3,750; total customer pays = N53,750. You collect N3,750 as output VAT and remit it to FIRS less any input VAT.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: When do I pay VAT to government?
Answer_EN: By the 21st of the following month. January supplies are remitted by February 21st, February by March 21st, and so on. Late remittance attracts penalty.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: Can I avoid VAT registration?
Answer_EN: If your turnover is below N25m annually, you are not required to register. However, if you exceed this threshold, registration becomes mandatory and failure to register attracts penalties.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: Do I pay VAT on services?
Answer_EN: Yes. Most services are taxable at 7.5% VAT. Exempt services include medical services, educational tuition, shared road transport, and financial services related to money and securities.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: What if I forget to remit VAT?
Answer_EN: Late or missed VAT remittance attracts a 10% penalty on the unremitted amount plus CBN interest rate from the due date. File and remit as soon as possible to minimize penalties.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: Can I get VAT refund?
Answer_EN: Yes. If your input VAT exceeds output VAT for the month, you can apply to FIRS for a refund or carry the excess forward as a credit against future VAT liabilities.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: I make money online do I need to pay tax?
Answer_EN: Yes. All online income is taxable in Nigeria. Register for Direct Assessment with your State IRS, declare all earnings annually by March 31st, and pay the assessed tax.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: Do they track my online income?
Answer_EN: Financial institutions and payment processors increasingly share data with tax authorities. FIRS also receives data from international platforms. The risk of detection is increasing as digital transaction monitoring improves.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: Do I pay tax on crypto profits?
Answer_EN: Yes. Crypto profits are subject to Capital Gains Tax in Nigeria. Each disposal of cryptocurrency at a profit creates a taxable gain to be declared in your annual return.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: Is my YouTube income taxable?
Answer_EN: Yes. YouTube AdSense earnings are taxable self-employment income. Convert USD/other currency earnings to Naira at the CBN rate on the payment date and declare in your annual PIT return.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: I earn from abroad do I pay tax here?
Answer_EN: Yes. As a Nigerian tax resident, all foreign earnings are taxable in Nigeria. Exceptions apply to dividends/rent brought through approved channels. Convert to Naira at CBN rate for tax purposes.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: Do they tax my freelance work online?
Answer_EN: Yes. Online freelance income (Fiverr, Upwork, Toptal, direct clients) is taxable as self-employment income. Register for Direct Assessment and declare all earnings annually.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: How do I report online income?
Answer_EN: File your annual PIT return with your State IRS. Under 'self-employment income' or 'other income', declare all online earnings. Convert foreign currency to Naira at the CBN rate on each transaction date.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: Can I hide online earnings?
Answer_EN: No. The NTA 2025 requires financial institutions to report certain transactions. FIRS also receives data from international platforms. Hiding income is tax evasion carrying fines up to N10m and imprisonment.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: Do they monitor PayPal income?

Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: Will I be taxed for remote work?
Answer_EN: Yes, if you are a Nigerian resident working remotely for any employer. Pay PIT through Direct Assessment at your State IRS. Your foreign employer does not need to register in Nigeria for PAYE purposes.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: Am I exempt from tax?
Answer_EN: You are exempt if: you earn at or below minimum wage, receive approved pension income, are a member of the armed forces (on salary), or your income falls entirely within an exempt category.

Question_EN: Do students like me pay tax?

Keywords: exempt, relief, pension, minimum wage, NGO, deduction, N800k

Question_EN: I am not earning much do I pay tax?

Question_EN: Do I qualify for tax relief?
Answer_EN: You qualify for: pension relief (8% of basic), NHF (2.5%), NHIS, life insurance premiums, and rent relief (20% of rent, max N500k). All require documentary evidence when filing.
Keywords: exempt, relief, pension, minimum wage, NGO, deduction, N800k
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 162 & Fifth Schedule

Question_EN: How do I reduce my tax?

Keywords: exempt, relief, pension, minimum wage, NGO, deduction, N800k
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 162 & Fifth Schedule

Question_EN: Can I claim deductions?

Keywords: exempt, relief, pension, minimum wage, NGO, deduction, N800k
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 162 & Fifth Schedule

Question_EN: What income can I exclude?
Answer_EN: Excludable income: pension (approved scheme), military salary, government bond interest, disability compensation (up to N50m), foreign dividends/rent brought through approved channels, and agricultural income (first 5 years).
Keywords: exempt, relief, pension, minimum wage, NGO, deduction, N800k
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 162 & Fifth Schedule

Question_EN: Do I pay tax on gifts I receive?
Answer_EN: Generally no. Gifts received are not taxable in your hands. However, very large or regular gifts may be viewed as income by the tax authority. Consult a tax professional for significant gifts.
Keywords: exempt, relief, pension, minimum wage, NGO, deduction, N800k

Question_EN: Are there ways to lower my tax?

Question_EN: I just got a job do I need to pay tax now?

Keywords: compliance, first time, new, voluntary disclosure, payment plan, TIN

Question_EN: Can I fix my tax problems now?

Question_EN: Can I clear my tax debt gradually?

Question_EN: I forgot to pay tax last year what happens?

Keywords: compliance, first time, new, voluntary disclosure, payment plan, TIN

Question_EN: Is it too late to start paying tax?

Keywords: compliance, first time, new, voluntary disclosure, payment plan, TIN
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025 — General Compliance Framework

Question_EN: Can I start fresh with my taxes?

Keywords: compliance, first time, new, voluntary disclosure, payment plan, TIN

Question_EN: How do I become fully compliant?
Answer_EN: Steps: 1. Register for TIN. 2. File all outstanding returns. 3. Pay all assessed taxes (instalment if needed). 4. Set up a system for ongoing record-keeping. 5. File and pay on time every year.

Question_EN: What is the Pay-As-You-Earn system under Nigerian tax law?
Answer_EN: PAYE is a statutory tax deduction system under the NTA 2025 whereby employers deduct income tax from employees' gross emoluments monthly and remit to the employee's State IRS.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: How is PAYE administered within the Nigerian tax framework?
Answer_EN: PAYE (Pay As You Earn) is a tax deduction system where employers deduct income tax from employees' salaries monthly and remit it directly to the relevant State Internal Revenue Service.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: Who bears the responsibility for PAYE remittance?
Answer_EN: The employer bears full legal responsibility for deducting and remitting PAYE. Failure attracts a 10% penalty plus CBN interest on the unremitted amount.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: What statutory deductions are included in PAYE calculations?
Answer_EN: Statutory deductions include: Pension (8% of basic salary), NHF (2.5% of basic salary), NHIS contributions, and life insurance premiums. These reduce the chargeable income before PAYE is applied.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: How is PAYE liability computed for employees?
Answer_EN: Annual Gross Income minus statutory deductions (Pension + NHF + NHIS + life insurance + rent relief) = Chargeable Income. Apply progressive tax bands. Divide annual tax by 12 for monthly PAYE.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: What are the employer's obligations under the PAYE system?
Answer_EN: Employers must: register with State IRS, deduct PAYE monthly, remit by the 10th of the following month, file monthly returns, issue annual tax deduction certificates, and maintain PAYE records for 6 years.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: What penalties apply for failure to remit PAYE?
Answer_EN: Employers who fail to remit PAYE face a penalty of 10% of the unremitted amount plus interest at the prevailing CBN Monetary Policy Rate per annum under the NTA 2025.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: How can employees verify PAYE deductions?
Answer_EN: Check payslips monthly, request annual employer certificate of deduction, verify through your State IRS portal using TIN, or engage a tax professional to review your PAYE records.

Question_EN: Is PAYE applicable to all categories of employees?

Question_EN: What regulatory body oversees PAYE compliance?

Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: What constitutes taxable income under the Personal Income Tax Act?
Answer_EN: Taxable income includes all income accruing to a resident individual: employment income, business profits, investment income (dividends, interest, royalties), rental income, capital gains, and digital income.

Question_EN: Who qualifies as a taxable individual in Nigeria?
Answer_EN: Any individual resident in Nigeria (domiciled, permanent home, or present 183+ days) who earns income above the minimum wage threshold is a taxable individual under the NTA 2025.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: How is personal income tax liability determined?
Answer_EN: PIT liability = (Gross Income minus allowable deductions) multiplied by applicable progressive tax rates. The resulting annual tax is divided by 12 for monthly PAYE deductions.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: What deductions are allowable under Nigerian tax law?
Answer_EN: Allowable deductions include: pension contributions (8% of basic salary), NHF (2.5%), NHIS, life insurance premiums, and rent relief (20% of rent paid, maximum N500,000).
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: What income streams are exempt from taxation?

Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: How are allowances treated for tax purposes?
Answer_EN: Most employment allowances are included in gross income for PAYE calculation. Benefits-in-kind are valued and added to taxable income. Only specific employer-provided items (uniforms, tools) are excluded.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: What is the applicable tax rate for individuals?
Answer_EN: Progressive rates apply: 0% on first N800,000; 15% on N800k-N3m; 18% on N3m-N12m; 21% on N12m-N25m; 23% on N25m-N50m; 25% on amounts exceeding N50m per annum.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: Are self-employed individuals subject to income tax?
Answer_EN: Yes. Self-employed individuals pay PIT through Direct Assessment with their State IRS. They must file annual returns by March 31st and pay tax on net business profits.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: How is residency determined for tax purposes?
Answer_EN: A person is a tax resident in Nigeria if they are domiciled there, have a permanent home available, sojourn for 183+ days in a 12-month period, or serve as a Nigerian diplomatic officer abroad.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: What provisions govern income earned from multiple sources?
Answer_EN: Under NTA 2025, all income from multiple sources is aggregated and taxed jointly as total income. The progressive tax bands apply to the combined chargeable income from all sources.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: What is the statutory requirement for filing tax returns?

Question_EN: What are the consequences of late filing?

Question_EN: Can tax returns be filed electronically in Nigeria?

Question_EN: What documentation must accompany a tax return?

Question_EN: What is the process for amending a submitted tax return?

Question_EN: What compliance standards govern tax filing?

Question_EN: What does tax compliance entail under Nigerian law?

Question_EN: What obligations must taxpayers fulfill to remain compliant?

Question_EN: What records are required for tax compliance?

Question_EN: How long must taxpayers retain financial records?

Question_EN: What is the scope of a tax audit?
