Keywords: compliance, records, audit, 6 years, FIRS, obligations, TIN

Question_EN: Under what conditions can a taxpayer be audited?
Answer_EN: A taxpayer can be audited: randomly, upon detection of risk indicators, based on third-party information, following non-filing, when returns appear inconsistent, or during sector-wide compliance exercises.
Keywords: compliance, records, audit, 6 years, FIRS, obligations, TIN

Question_EN: What triggers a tax audit in Nigeria?
Answer_EN: Triggers include: inconsistent tax returns, lifestyle exceeding declared income, sector compliance reviews, third-party data matching, non-filing, large or unusual transactions, and random audit selection.
Keywords: compliance, records, audit, 6 years, FIRS, obligations, TIN

Question_EN: What procedures are followed during a tax audit?
Answer_EN: Procedures: advance notice, document request list, document submission by taxpayer, auditor's review, preliminary findings meeting, taxpayer's objection/response, revised findings, and final assessment.
Keywords: compliance, records, audit, 6 years, FIRS, obligations, TIN

Question_EN: What rights do taxpayers have during an audit?
Answer_EN: Rights include: advance notice of audit, right to professional representation, access to audit findings, opportunity to respond to preliminary findings, right to object to incorrect assessments, and right to appeal.
Keywords: compliance, records, audit, 6 years, FIRS, obligations, TIN

Question_EN: What documentation is required during an audit?
Answer_EN: Required: all returns for the audit period, bank statements, income records, expense receipts, payroll schedules, VAT returns and invoices, asset schedules, contracts, and WHT certificates.
Keywords: compliance, records, audit, 6 years, FIRS, obligations, TIN

Question_EN: What penalties apply for failure to pay tax?
Answer_EN: Penalty of 10% of the unpaid amount plus interest at the CBN Monetary Policy Rate per annum from the due date. Persistent non-payment leads to enforcement action.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: What sanctions are imposed for late tax filing?
Answer_EN: N50,000 for the first month of default and N25,000 for each subsequent month until the return is filed. Interest on any unpaid tax also accrues from the due date.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: What constitutes tax evasion under Nigerian law?
Answer_EN: Tax evasion includes: deliberately omitting income from returns, providing false or misleading information, failing to register for tax when required, and colluding with others to defeat tax laws.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: What legal consequences arise from tax evasion?
Answer_EN: Legal consequences: criminal prosecution, fines up to N10 million, imprisonment up to 5 years, forfeiture of assets representing proceeds of evasion, and recovery of all evaded taxes with interest and penalties.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: How are penalties for late payment calculated?
Answer_EN: Late payment penalty = 10% of the unpaid tax. Interest = CBN Monetary Policy Rate per annum on the unpaid amount from the due date. Both apply simultaneously until full payment.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: Can tax penalties be waived under certain conditions?
Answer_EN: Yes. Penalties may be waived for: first-time offenders, force majeure events, genuine financial hardship with supporting evidence, or when voluntary disclosure is made before detection.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: What interest is charged on unpaid tax liabilities?
Answer_EN: Interest is charged at the prevailing CBN Monetary Policy Rate per annum on all unpaid tax from the due date. The rate changes as CBN adjusts its benchmark rate.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: What enforcement actions can be taken against defaulters?
Answer_EN: Enforcement actions include: demand notices, bank account restrictions, garnishment of debtors, seizure and sale of assets, prosecution in court, and travel restrictions in extreme cases.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: Is there a legal process for appealing tax penalties?
Answer_EN: Yes. Object to the assessment within 30 days. If objection fails, appeal to the Tax Appeal Tribunal within 30 days. Further appeal lies to the Federal High Court on questions of law.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: What remedies are available to taxpayers facing penalties?
Answer_EN: Remedies include: objection to assessment, appeal to Tax Appeal Tribunal, application for penalty waiver, application for instalment payment plan, and voluntary disclosure to reduce penalty exposure.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: What is the Value Added Tax framework in Nigeria?
Answer_EN: The VAT framework under the NTA 2025 imposes 7.5% on taxable supplies, exempts specified goods/services, zero-rates essentials, and requires registration, monthly filing, and remittance by VAT-registered persons.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: What is the current VAT rate applicable in Nigeria?
Answer_EN: The current VAT rate is 7.5% on all taxable supplies. Essential goods (food, medicine, education) are zero-rated at 0%. Specified items (land, buildings, money) are fully exempt.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: Who is required to charge and remit VAT?
Answer_EN: All VAT-registered persons (annual turnover >N25m or voluntary registrants) are required to charge 7.5% VAT on taxable supplies and remit net VAT to FIRS by the 21st of the following month.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: What goods and services are exempt from VAT?
Answer_EN: Exempt: land and buildings, money/securities, government licences, military hardware, baby products (0-3 years), shared road transport, plays by educational institutions, and assistive devices for the disabled.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: How is VAT calculated on taxable supplies?
Answer_EN: VAT = taxable supply value x 7.5%. For imported goods: VAT = (CIF value + import duties + other charges) x 7.5%. Output VAT - Input VAT = net VAT payable to FIRS.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: What is the difference between input VAT and output VAT?
Answer_EN: Output VAT is VAT charged on your sales (collected from customers). Input VAT is VAT paid on your purchases. The difference (Output - Input) is remitted to FIRS or claimed as a refund.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: What are the timelines for VAT remittance?
Answer_EN: VAT must be remitted by the 21st day of the month following the transaction month. Annual VAT returns are also required within 90 days of the end of the accounting year.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: What compliance requirements apply to VAT-registered entities?
Answer_EN: Compliance requirements: issue valid VAT invoices with sequential numbering, maintain VAT accounts, file monthly VAT returns, remit net VAT by 21st of following month, and keep records for 6 years.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: Can VAT be reclaimed under Nigerian tax law?
Answer_EN: Yes. Excess input VAT can be claimed as a refund or credit. Businesses must apply to FIRS with VAT returns and supporting invoices. Refunds are due within 90 days of application.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: What penalties apply for non-remittance of VAT?
Answer_EN: Failure to remit VAT attracts a penalty of 10% of the unremitted amount plus interest at the CBN rate. Persistent non-remittance can lead to prosecution and criminal sanctions.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: What tax obligations apply to self-employed individuals?
Answer_EN: Obligations: TIN registration, annual PIT return filing by March 31st, payment of assessed tax, VAT registration if turnover exceeds N25m, WHT deduction where applicable, and 6-year record keeping.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: How is taxable income determined for small businesses?
Answer_EN: Taxable income = Total business revenue minus allowable deductions (business expenses, capital allowances, WHT paid). For sole traders, further statutory deductions (pension, NHF) reduce the chargeable income.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: Are informal sector operators subject to taxation?
Answer_EN: Yes. Informal sector operators (market traders, artisans, transport workers) are subject to presumptive tax administered by State IRS. The specific amount is assessed based on business type and location.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: What registration requirements apply to small enterprises?
Answer_EN: Small enterprises must: register with CAC (for formal businesses), obtain TIN from FIRS/JTB, register with State IRS for PIT, and register for VAT if annual turnover exceeds N25m.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: How are business profits taxed in Nigeria?
Answer_EN: Sole trader profits are taxed as PIT at progressive rates (0%–25%). Company profits are taxed as CIT: 0% (small companies, turnover <N100m), 20% (medium), or 30% (large).
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: What records must small businesses maintain?
Answer_EN: Maintain: daily income/sales records, expense receipts and invoices, bank statements, payroll records, asset purchase documents, VAT invoices (if registered), and contracts with clients.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: What tax regime applies to startups?
Answer_EN: Registered startup companies with turnover below N100m pay 0% CIT. Labelled startups under the Startup Act receive additional incentives. All must file returns and maintain compliance.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: How is presumptive tax applied in Nigeria?
Answer_EN: Presumptive tax is applied by State IRS to informal sector businesses where records are unavailable. Tax is based on benchmarks such as turnover estimates, business size, or industry norms set by the Minister.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: Are digital entrepreneurs subject to taxation?
Answer_EN: Yes. Digital entrepreneurs (app developers, content creators, e-commerce operators) are subject to PIT or CIT on their income. Foreign currency earnings must be converted to Naira at CBN rate.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: What reporting requirements apply to business owners?
Answer_EN: Business owners must: file annual income tax returns, submit monthly VAT returns (if registered), file monthly PAYE returns (if employing staff), and maintain records for 6 years.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: Who qualifies for tax exemptions under Nigerian law?
Answer_EN: Qualifying parties include: minimum wage earners, pension fund beneficiaries, approved charities and NGOs, co-operative societies, religious organisations (non-trading), government institutions, and agricultural businesses (first 5 years).
Keywords: exempt, relief, pension, minimum wage, NGO, deduction, N800k
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 162 & Fifth Schedule

Question_EN: What categories of income are tax-exempt?
Answer_EN: Tax-exempt income: minimum wage earnings, approved pension, military salary, government bond interest, disability compensation (up to N50m), foreign dividends/rent via approved channels, and new agricultural business income.
Keywords: exempt, relief, pension, minimum wage, NGO, deduction, N800k
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 162 & Fifth Schedule

Question_EN: What is the nature of tax relief allowances?
Answer_EN: Tax relief allowances reduce chargeable income before tax is calculated. They include pension contributions, NHF, NHIS, life insurance premiums, and rent relief — all proven by documentary evidence.
Keywords: exempt, relief, pension, minimum wage, NGO, deduction, N800k
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 162 & Fifth Schedule

Question_EN: How can taxpayers claim reliefs and deductions?
Answer_EN: Claim reliefs by submitting supporting documents (pension statements, NHF receipts, NHIS certificates, rent receipts, life insurance policy) when filing the annual PIT return with your State IRS.
Keywords: exempt, relief, pension, minimum wage, NGO, deduction, N800k
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 162 & Fifth Schedule

Question_EN: Are charitable organizations exempt from taxation?
Answer_EN: Yes. Charitable organisations of public character are exempt from income tax on non-commercial income. They must register, file nil returns, and not distribute profits to members.
Keywords: exempt, relief, pension, minimum wage, NGO, deduction, N800k
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 162 & Fifth Schedule

Question_EN: What provisions apply to pension income?
Answer_EN: Approved pension income is fully exempt from PIT. Contributions to approved pension schemes are also tax-deductible (8% of basic salary). These provisions are under the Pension Reform Act 2014.
Keywords: exempt, relief, pension, minimum wage, NGO, deduction, N800k
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 162 & Fifth Schedule

Question_EN: Are there exemptions for persons with disabilities?
Answer_EN: Yes. Disability/personal injury compensation up to N50m is exempt from CGT. VAT is waived on assistive devices. Employers get additional deductions for costs incurred on disability-related products.
Keywords: exempt, relief, pension, minimum wage, NGO, deduction, N800k
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 162 & Fifth Schedule

Question_EN: How are gifts treated under tax law?
Answer_EN: Gifts are not directly taxable for the recipient. However, the donor may be subject to CGT on gains from gifted assets. Gifts of property are subject to stamp duty based on market value.
Keywords: exempt, relief, pension, minimum wage, NGO, deduction, N800k
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 162 & Fifth Schedule

Question_EN: What legal provisions govern tax exemptions?
Answer_EN: Tax exemptions are governed by: Section 162 of the NTA 2025 (income tax exemptions), Section 186 (VAT exemptions), the Fourth Schedule (tax rates and exemptions), and the Fifth Schedule (settlements and trusts).
Keywords: exempt, relief, pension, minimum wage, NGO, deduction, N800k
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 162 & Fifth Schedule

Question_EN: What limitations apply to tax relief claims?
Answer_EN: Limitations: pension relief is capped at 8% of basic salary, rent relief is capped at N500,000, NHF at 2.5% of basic salary. All reliefs require documentary evidence. Unsubstantiated claims may be disallowed.
Keywords: exempt, relief, pension, minimum wage, NGO, deduction, N800k
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 162 & Fifth Schedule

Question_EN: How is digital income treated under Nigerian tax law?
Answer_EN: Digital income from all sources (social media, crypto, e-commerce, streaming, freelancing) is taxable in Nigeria. Residents declare all digital income in their annual PIT return. Non-residents on Nigerian-sourced digital income are also taxed.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: Are foreign digital service providers subject to taxation?
Answer_EN: Yes. Foreign digital service providers with Significant Economic Presence (SEP) in Nigeria — earning from Nigerian users — must register with FIRS and pay tax on their Nigeria-attributable income.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: What tax obligations apply to online businesses?
Answer_EN: Online businesses must: register for TIN, file annual PIT (sole trader) or CIT (company) returns, register for VAT if turnover exceeds N25m, charge VAT on taxable digital supplies, and declare all income.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: How is income from remote work taxed?
Answer_EN: Nigerian residents doing remote work for foreign employers pay PIT through Direct Assessment at their State IRS. Income is converted to Naira at the CBN rate. The foreign employer need not register in Nigeria.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: What reporting requirements apply to digital earnings?
Answer_EN: All digital earnings must be included in the annual PIT return. Keep records of all transactions (platform statements, payment receipts, bank records showing deposits). Convert FX at CBN rates.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: How are cryptocurrency transactions taxed?
Answer_EN: Each crypto disposal (sale, swap, use as payment) is a potentially taxable event. Gains (proceeds minus cost basis) are subject to CGT at progressive PIT rates. Mining income is taxed as business income.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: What regulations govern e-commerce taxation?
Answer_EN: E-commerce is taxed under standard PIT (sole traders) or CIT (registered companies). VAT at 7.5% applies to taxable goods/services sold online. Foreign e-commerce platforms need SEP registration for Nigerian revenue.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: Are social media earnings subject to tax?
Answer_EN: Yes. All earnings from social media platforms (sponsored content, affiliate links, brand deals, platform monetization) are taxable self-employment income that must be declared in your annual PIT return.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: What compliance measures apply to digital platforms?
Answer_EN: Nigerian digital platforms must: register for CIT/PIT, charge VAT on taxable supplies, deduct WHT on payments to suppliers, file all tax returns, and maintain financial records for 6 years.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: How does Nigeria regulate cross-border digital taxation?
Answer_EN: Nigeria uses the Significant Economic Presence (SEP) rule to tax foreign digital companies earning from Nigerian users. This is consistent with OECD Pillar One principles on taxing the digital economy.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: Can you explain how taxation works in Nigeria?
Answer_EN: In Nigeria, individuals pay PIT to their State IRS, companies pay CIT to FIRS, and businesses collect VAT on behalf of FIRS. All taxable persons must register, file returns, and pay within legal deadlines.
Keywords: tax, Nigeria, FIRS, NRS, law, 2025, NTA

Question_EN: What are the main types of taxes in Nigeria?
Answer_EN: Main taxes: Personal Income Tax (salary/business income), Company Income Tax (company profits), VAT (goods/services), Withholding Tax (deducted at source), Capital Gains Tax (asset disposal), and Stamp Duty (documents).
Keywords: tax, Nigeria, FIRS, NRS, law, 2025, NTA

Question_EN: Why is taxation important for the country?
Answer_EN: Taxation is the primary source of government revenue. Without tax, there would be no funding for roads, schools, hospitals, defence, and social programmes that sustain the country.
Keywords: tax, Nigeria, FIRS, NRS, law, 2025, NTA

Question_EN: Who is responsible for collecting taxes in Nigeria?
Answer_EN: FIRS/NRS collects federal taxes (CIT, VAT, WHT, Development Levy). State Internal Revenue Services (KIRS, LIRS, etc.) collect PIT from individuals and businesses in their states.
Keywords: tax, Nigeria, FIRS, NRS, law, 2025, NTA

Question_EN: What has changed in the recent tax reforms?
Answer_EN: Key 2025 changes: 0% CIT for small companies, 0% PIT for minimum wage earners, new 4% Development Levy, rent relief allowance (N500k max), digital income now taxed, and all major tax laws consolidated into one Act.
Keywords: tax, Nigeria, FIRS, NRS, law, 2025, NTA

Question_EN: How does tax affect individuals in Nigeria?
Answer_EN: Tax reduces disposable income but funds essential services. Low earners are protected through exemptions (0% on first N800k). Compliance is rewarded with Tax Clearance Certificates needed for business and government contracts.
Keywords: tax, Nigeria, FIRS, NRS, law, 2025, NTA

Question_EN: What is the role of government in taxation?
Answer_EN: The government sets tax rates, collects revenue through NRS/FIRS and State IRS, enforces compliance, grants incentives, and uses tax proceeds to fund national development.
Keywords: tax, Nigeria, FIRS, NRS, law, 2025, NTA

Question_EN: Are taxes the same across all states?
Answer_EN: No. Federal taxes (CIT, VAT) are uniform nationwide. But state taxes like PIT rates and levies vary slightly by state. Each state has its own IRS (KIRS, LIRS, BIRS, etc.).
Keywords: tax, Nigeria, FIRS, NRS, law, 2025, NTA

Question_EN: How is tax policy implemented in Nigeria?
Answer_EN: Tax policy is formulated by the Federal Ministry of Finance, implemented by the NRS/FIRS and State IRS offices, and governed by the Nigeria Tax Act 2025 and related legislation.
Keywords: tax, Nigeria, FIRS, NRS, law, 2025, NTA

Question_EN: What should every citizen know about tax?
Answer_EN: Every citizen should know: register for TIN, file annual returns, pay tax by deadlines, keep records for 6 years, know your exemptions and reliefs, and report unfair treatment to the Tax Ombudsman.
Keywords: tax, Nigeria, FIRS, NRS, law, 2025, NTA

Question_EN: How can I register for a Tax Identification Number?
Answer_EN: A Tax Identification Number (TIN) is a unique number assigned to every taxpayer by the Nigeria Revenue Service (NRS) for tax identification and compliance purposes.
Keywords: TIN, Tax ID, register, FIRS, NIN, JTB, online

Question_EN: Is it necessary to have a TIN before working?
Answer_EN: Yes. Your employer is required to have your TIN before processing your first salary to ensure PAYE is correctly remitted to your State IRS.
Keywords: TIN, Tax ID, register, FIRS, NIN, JTB, online

Question_EN: Where can I apply for a TIN in Nigeria?
Answer_EN: Apply online at tin.jtb.gov.ng, or visit FIRS offices in Abuja, Lagos, Kano, Port Harcourt, and other major cities, or your State Internal Revenue Service office.
Keywords: TIN, Tax ID, register, FIRS, NIN, JTB, online

Question_EN: Can I complete TIN registration online?
Answer_EN: Yes, fully. Visit tin.jtb.gov.ng, provide your NIN, personal details, and email. Your TIN is emailed within 24-48 hours. No physical visit required for standard registration.
Keywords: TIN, Tax ID, register, FIRS, NIN, JTB, online

Question_EN: How long does it take to get a TIN?
Answer_EN: A Tax Identification Number (TIN) is a unique number assigned to every taxpayer by the Nigeria Revenue Service (NRS) for tax identification and compliance purposes.
Keywords: TIN, Tax ID, register, FIRS, NIN, JTB, online

Question_EN: Can I update my TIN details later?
Answer_EN: Yes. Contact your State IRS or FIRS with your updated information and supporting documents. Changes to address and contact details are straightforward; identity changes require official documents.
Keywords: TIN, Tax ID, register, FIRS, NIN, JTB, online

Question_EN: What happens if I don't have a TIN?
Answer_EN: Without a TIN: banks may restrict your account, government contracts become inaccessible, employers cannot properly process PAYE, and you face penalties of N50,000+ for non-registration.
Keywords: TIN, Tax ID, register, FIRS, NIN, JTB, online

Question_EN: Is TIN required for small businesses?
Answer_EN: Yes. All businesses regardless of size must register for TIN. Small businesses also need TIN to open business bank accounts and receive payments from companies.
Keywords: TIN, Tax ID, register, FIRS, NIN, JTB, online

Question_EN: How does the PAYE system operate in Nigeria?
Answer_EN: Employers register with the relevant State IRS, calculate each employee's annual tax, deduct monthly instalments from gross salary, remit to State IRS by 10th of following month, and file monthly returns.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: Why is tax deducted from my salary?
Answer_EN: PAYE (Pay As You Earn) is a tax deduction system where employers deduct income tax from employees' salaries monthly and remit it directly to the relevant State Internal Revenue Service.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: How can I verify my PAYE deductions?
Answer_EN: PAYE (Pay As You Earn) is a tax deduction system where employers deduct income tax from employees' salaries monthly and remit it directly to the relevant State Internal Revenue Service.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: Who is responsible for PAYE remittance?
Answer_EN: The employer is solely responsible for PAYE remittance. This is a non-negotiable legal duty. Even if the employer faces financial difficulties, PAYE must be remitted on time.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: Can I request details of my PAYE calculation?
Answer_EN: Yes. You have the right to request a detailed breakdown of your PAYE calculation from your employer. They must provide this in writing, including your gross income and all deductions.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: Is PAYE applicable to all employees?
Answer_EN: PAYE applies to all employees earning above the minimum wage threshold. Those earning exactly at or below the National Minimum Wage are exempt from PIT and thus PAYE.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: What should I do if PAYE is not deducted properly?
Answer_EN: First, inform your employer's payroll/HR department in writing. If unresolved within 30 days, file a formal complaint with your State IRS, providing your payslips as evidence.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: How often is PAYE deducted from salary?
Answer_EN: PAYE is deducted monthly from your salary. Your employer calculates the monthly equivalent of your annual tax liability and deducts it from each monthly payroll.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: Can I get a refund for excess PAYE deductions?
Answer_EN: Yes. File a refund claim with your State IRS after the tax year ends. Provide payslips, your TIN, employer PAYE schedule, and bank details. Refunds must be processed within 90 days.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: How do I raise a complaint about PAYE?
Answer_EN: Write to your State IRS (e.g., KIRS for Kano, LIRS for Lagos) with your TIN, employer details, payslips, and description of the issue. You can also use the Tax Ombudsman office.
Keywords: PAYE, salary, employer, deduction, State IRS, monthly, remit
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 13; PAYE Regulations 2025

Question_EN: What is considered taxable income in Nigeria?
Answer_EN: Taxable income is all income accruing to an individual from employment, self-employment, investments, rental properties, digital platforms, and any other source, minus allowable deductions.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: Who is required to pay personal income tax?
Answer_EN: All resident individuals earning income above the minimum wage threshold, including employees, self-employed persons, freelancers, property owners, investors, and business partners.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: Are allowances included in taxable income?
Answer_EN: Yes. Employment allowances (housing, transport, feeding, utility) form part of gross employment income and are included in the chargeable income before PAYE is calculated.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: Do freelancers need to pay income tax?
Answer_EN: Yes. Freelancers are self-employed individuals liable to PIT. They must register with their State IRS, file annual returns, and pay assessed tax by March 31st each year.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: Is income from multiple sources taxable?
Answer_EN: Yes. All income sources are aggregated. Whether from salary, rent, freelancing, or investments, all income is combined and taxed using the progressive PIT bands after allowable deductions.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: What deductions can reduce my tax liability?
Answer_EN: Deductions include: pension (8% of basic), NHF (2.5%), NHIS, life insurance premiums, and rent relief (20% of rent paid, max N500,000). These reduce your chargeable income directly.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: How do I determine my tax bracket?
Answer_EN: Calculate your total annual income minus allowable deductions. Your chargeable income falls into one or more progressive bands: 0% (≤N800k), 15% (N800k-N3m), 18% (N3m-N12m), 21%, 23%, or 25%.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: Are bonuses and incentives taxed?
Answer_EN: Yes. Bonuses, 13th month salary, performance incentives, and other lump-sum payments are taxable employment income. They are added to monthly salary and taxed at your marginal rate.
Keywords: PIT, income, salary, individual, tax bands, N800k, progressive
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Sections 12–31 & Fourth Schedule

Question_EN: How can I file my tax returns in Nigeria?
Answer_EN: File online at efiling.firs.gov.ng (for CIT/VAT) or your State IRS portal (for PIT). Walk-in filing is also available at FIRS and State IRS offices nationwide.
Keywords: filing, return, deadline, March 31, e-filing, annual, online

Question_EN: When is the deadline for filing tax returns?
Answer_EN: Individuals must file by March 31st annually. Companies must file within 6 months after the end of their accounting year. Late filing attracts penalties.
Keywords: filing, return, deadline, March 31, e-filing, annual, online

Question_EN: Can tax returns be submitted online?
Answer_EN: Yes, fully. Both FIRS (efiling.firs.gov.ng) and most State IRS offices have online filing portals. Online filing is encouraged as it is faster and provides instant acknowledgment.
Keywords: filing, return, deadline, March 31, e-filing, annual, online

Question_EN: What happens if I file my taxes late?
Answer_EN: You face a penalty of N50,000 for the first month of late filing and N25,000 for each subsequent month, plus interest on any unpaid tax at the CBN Monetary Policy Rate.
Keywords: filing, return, deadline, March 31, e-filing, annual, online

Question_EN: Can I correct an error in my tax return?
Answer_EN: Yes. File an amended return within 6 years. Corrections made voluntarily before an audit are generally treated more favourably than those discovered during enforcement.
Keywords: filing, return, deadline, March 31, e-filing, annual, online

Question_EN: Is it necessary to use an accountant for filing?
Answer_EN: Not mandatory for simple cases. However, for businesses, multiple income sources, or complex deductions, a qualified accountant ensures accuracy, maximizes legitimate deductions, and reduces audit risk.
Keywords: filing, return, deadline, March 31, e-filing, annual, online

Question_EN: How often should I file my tax returns?
Answer_EN: Once a year for PIT (by March 31st) and CIT (within 6 months of year-end). Monthly for VAT and PAYE. Quarterly instalment payments apply for companies with estimated tax liabilities.
Keywords: filing, return, deadline, March 31, e-filing, annual, online

Question_EN: Can someone file taxes on my behalf?
Answer_EN: Yes. With a written authority letter or Power of Attorney, an authorized agent (accountant, tax consultant) can file on your behalf. You remain legally responsible for return accuracy.
Keywords: filing, return, deadline, March 31, e-filing, annual, online

Question_EN: What steps are involved in filing tax returns?
Answer_EN: 1. Gather all income and deduction documents. 2. Calculate chargeable income. 3. Log into e-filing portal. 4. Complete return form. 5. Upload supporting documents. 6. Submit. 7. Pay assessed tax. 8. Download receipt.
Keywords: filing, return, deadline, March 31, e-filing, annual, online

Question_EN: What does it mean to be tax compliant?
Answer_EN: Being tax compliant means meeting all your legal tax obligations: registered for TIN, filed all returns on time, paid all taxes assessed, deducted WHT where required, and maintained proper records.
Keywords: compliance, records, audit, 6 years, FIRS, obligations, TIN

Question_EN: How can I ensure I comply with tax regulations?
Answer_EN: Ensure compliance by: maintaining a tax calendar (deadlines), keeping organized records, engaging a tax professional, filing returns early, paying taxes on time, and responding to all correspondence.
Keywords: compliance, records, audit, 6 years, FIRS, obligations, TIN

Question_EN: What records should I keep for tax purposes?
Answer_EN: For tax purposes, keep: income statements, payslips, invoices, receipts, contracts, bank statements, pension and NHF contribution records, asset purchase documents, and all tax-related correspondence.
Keywords: compliance, records, audit, 6 years, FIRS, obligations, TIN

Question_EN: How long should I retain my tax documents?
Answer_EN: Retain all tax documents for a minimum of 6 years after the relevant tax year. Electronic copies are acceptable. For suspected fraud, documents may be required beyond the 6-year period.
Keywords: compliance, records, audit, 6 years, FIRS, obligations, TIN

Question_EN: How do I prepare for a tax audit?
Answer_EN: Tax compliance means meeting all your tax obligations: registering for TIN, filing accurate returns by deadlines, paying assessed taxes on time, and keeping proper financial records for 6 years.
Keywords: compliance, records, audit, 6 years, FIRS, obligations, TIN

Question_EN: What can trigger a tax audit?
Answer_EN: Common triggers: inconsistent income declarations, significant cash transactions, lifestyle exceeding declared income, industry-wide audits, third-party reports, random selection, and failure to file returns.
Keywords: compliance, records, audit, 6 years, FIRS, obligations, TIN

Question_EN: Are small businesses subject to audits?
Answer_EN: Yes. All registered businesses are subject to tax audits. Small businesses may be selected randomly or specifically if their returns show inconsistencies or they operate in cash-heavy sectors.
Keywords: compliance, records, audit, 6 years, FIRS, obligations, TIN

Question_EN: What rights do I have during an audit?
Answer_EN: You have the right to: professional representation, adequate notice before audit, access to audit findings and methodology, submit responses to preliminary findings, and appeal any incorrect assessment.
Keywords: compliance, records, audit, 6 years, FIRS, obligations, TIN

Question_EN: How can I respond to audit requests?
Answer_EN: Acknowledge the audit notice, engage a tax professional, gather all requested documents, prepare detailed responses to each query, and submit everything within the stipulated timeline.
Keywords: compliance, records, audit, 6 years, FIRS, obligations, TIN

Question_EN: What penalties apply for late tax payment?
Answer_EN: 10% penalty on the unpaid tax amount, plus interest at the CBN Monetary Policy Rate per annum from the due date. Both accumulate until full payment is made.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: What happens if I fail to file my tax returns?
Answer_EN: Non-filing results in: N50,000 penalty (first month), N25,000 per subsequent month, possible estimated tax assessment by the authority, and escalating enforcement actions.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: Can penalties be reduced or waived?
Answer_EN: Yes, through: voluntary disclosure, demonstrating genuine hardship, first-time offence status, force majeure events, or full payment of the underlying tax with a formal waiver application.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: How is interest calculated on unpaid taxes?
Answer_EN: Interest = unpaid tax amount multiplied by the CBN Monetary Policy Rate per annum, calculated from the tax due date. The rate varies as CBN adjusts monetary policy.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: What actions can authorities take against defaulters?
Answer_EN: Actions include: demand notices, bank account restriction, attachment of third-party debtors, seizure and sale of moveable and immoveable assets, and criminal prosecution.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: Can I appeal a tax penalty decision?
Answer_EN: Yes. File an objection within 30 days of the penalty notice. If the objection is refused, appeal to the Tax Appeal Tribunal within 30 days of the objection decision.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: What are the consequences of tax evasion?
Answer_EN: Consequences: criminal prosecution, fines up to N10 million, imprisonment up to 5 years, public naming, asset forfeiture, and obligation to repay all evaded taxes plus penalties and interest.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: How do I resolve outstanding tax penalties?
Answer_EN: Contact your State IRS or FIRS, disclose all outstanding liabilities, pay the principal tax and negotiate penalty reduction, or apply for a structured payment plan under the NTA 2025.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: Is there a way to avoid tax penalties?
Answer_EN: Yes: file all returns on time, pay taxes by due dates, deduct and remit WHT correctly, keep accurate records, respond to audit notices promptly, and seek professional tax advice.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: What should I do if I receive a penalty notice?
Answer_EN: Respond within 30 days. Either pay the penalty, file an objection if you believe it is wrong, or apply for a waiver. Never ignore a penalty notice — silence is treated as acceptance.
Keywords: penalty, fine, N50k, late, evasion, prison, interest, CBN rate

Question_EN: How does VAT work in Nigeria?
Answer_EN: Businesses collect 7.5% VAT on taxable sales from customers (output VAT), deduct VAT paid on business purchases (input VAT), and remit the net difference to FIRS by the 21st of the following month.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: Who is required to charge VAT?
Answer_EN: VAT-registered persons (annual turnover >N25m or voluntary registrants) must charge VAT. All federal, state, and local government agencies must also withhold VAT on payments to suppliers.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: How do I calculate VAT on my sales?
Answer_EN: Multiply your taxable sale value by 7.5%. Issue a VAT invoice showing the pre-VAT amount and VAT separately. Collect both, remit the VAT to FIRS, and keep the pre-VAT amount as your revenue.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: When should VAT be remitted to the government?
Answer_EN: By the 21st day of the month following the transaction. January transactions: remit by February 21st. File the monthly VAT return simultaneously with remittance.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: What is the difference between input and output VAT?
Answer_EN: Output VAT: 7.5% you charge customers on your sales. Input VAT: 7.5% you paid on business purchases. Net VAT to FIRS = Output VAT minus Input VAT. If Input > Output, claim a refund.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: Do small businesses need to register for VAT?
Answer_EN: Mandatory only if annual turnover exceeds N25m. Below this threshold, registration is voluntary. However, unregistered businesses cannot claim input VAT credits on their purchases.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: Can VAT be reclaimed by businesses?
Answer_EN: Yes. VAT-registered businesses can deduct input VAT (VAT paid on business purchases) from output VAT (VAT collected from customers). If input exceeds output, apply to FIRS for a refund.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: What happens if VAT is not remitted?
Answer_EN: Non-remittance of VAT attracts: 10% penalty on unremitted amount, CBN interest rate from due date, possible prosecution, and revocation of VAT registration with consequential assessment of all unremitted VAT.
Keywords: VAT, 7.5%, input, output, exempt, zero-rated, FIRS, 21st

Question_EN: Do self-employed individuals need to pay tax?
Answer_EN: Yes. Self-employed individuals pay PIT through Direct Assessment at their State IRS. All business income minus allowable expenses is subject to PIT at progressive rates.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: How is tax calculated for small businesses?
Answer_EN: Net profit (revenue minus allowable expenses) is the taxable base. Apply progressive PIT rates for sole traders or 0% CIT for incorporated small companies with turnover below N100m.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: What tax obligations apply to freelancers?
Answer_EN: Self-employed individuals and small businesses pay Personal Income Tax (PIT) on business profits through Direct Assessment. They must register with their State IRS, declare income annually, and file by March 31st.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: Do I need to register my business for tax purposes?
Answer_EN: Yes. All businesses — sole traders, partnerships, and companies — must be registered for tax. Register for TIN at FIRS, with your State IRS for PIT, and with FIRS for VAT if applicable.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: How can I declare my business income?
Answer_EN: File your annual PIT return (sole trader) or CIT return (company) showing: total revenue, allowable expenses, capital allowances, net taxable income, and tax computed. Attach financial statements.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: What taxes apply to startups in Nigeria?
Answer_EN: Startups (incorporated companies, turnover <N100m): 0% CIT. Sole trader startups: PIT on profits. All startups should register for TIN and consider VAT registration if turnover exceeds N25m.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: Are online businesses required to pay tax?
Answer_EN: Yes. All online businesses earning income from Nigerian or foreign sources are required to pay tax. Income from e-commerce, digital services, freelancing, and content creation is taxable.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: How do I keep records for my business?
Answer_EN: Use accounting software (Wave, Zoho, QuickBooks) or maintain a spreadsheet: record all income and expenses daily, retain all invoices and receipts, reconcile with bank statements monthly.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: What happens if my business is not registered?
Answer_EN: Self-employed individuals and small businesses pay Personal Income Tax (PIT) on business profits through Direct Assessment. They must register with their State IRS, declare income annually, and file by March 31st.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: How can I stay compliant as a business owner?
Answer_EN: Compliance steps: register for TIN, file all returns on time, pay taxes by due dates, deduct and remit WHT where applicable, register for VAT if required, and maintain records for 6 years.
Keywords: self-employed, freelance, business, PIT, Direct Assessment, small business

Question_EN: Who qualifies for tax exemptions in Nigeria?
Answer_EN: Exempt persons include: minimum wage earners, approved pension recipients, military officers (on salary), religious/charitable organisations (non-trading income), co-operatives, government agencies, and agricultural businesses (first 5 years).
Keywords: exempt, relief, pension, minimum wage, NGO, deduction, N800k
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 162 & Fifth Schedule

Question_EN: What income is exempt from taxation?
Answer_EN: Exempt income: minimum wage earnings, approved pension, military salary, government bond interest, disability compensation (up to N50m), NGO non-commercial income, agricultural business income (first 5 years).
Keywords: exempt, relief, pension, minimum wage, NGO, deduction, N800k
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 162 & Fifth Schedule

Question_EN: How can I claim tax relief allowances?
Answer_EN: Gather documentation for each relief (pension statement, NHF receipt, NHIS card, rent agreement with receipts, life insurance certificate). Include these when filing your annual PIT return with your State IRS.
Keywords: exempt, relief, pension, minimum wage, NGO, deduction, N800k
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 162 & Fifth Schedule

Question_EN: Are students required to pay tax?
Answer_EN: Students without income are not required to pay tax. All adults (18+) must register for TIN. If a student earns income from part-time work, business, or online platforms, that income is taxable.
Keywords: exempt, relief, pension, minimum wage, NGO, deduction, N800k
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 162 & Fifth Schedule

Question_EN: Do retirees pay income tax?
Answer_EN: Retirees receiving income only from approved pension schemes are exempt from PIT. Retirees earning additional income from consulting, rent, or investments pay PIT on those additional earnings.
Keywords: exempt, relief, pension, minimum wage, NGO, deduction, N800k
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 162 & Fifth Schedule

Question_EN: What deductions are allowed under tax law?
Answer_EN: Allowable deductions: pension (8% of basic), NHF (2.5%), NHIS, life insurance premiums, rent relief (20% of rent, max N500k). For businesses: all expenses wholly and exclusively incurred for business.
Keywords: exempt, relief, pension, minimum wage, NGO, deduction, N800k
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 162 & Fifth Schedule

Question_EN: Are charitable organizations exempt from tax?
Answer_EN: Yes. Charitable, religious, and educational institutions of a public character are exempt from income tax on non-commercial activities. They must file returns and prove income is not commercially derived.
Keywords: exempt, relief, pension, minimum wage, NGO, deduction, N800k
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 162 & Fifth Schedule

Question_EN: How do tax reliefs reduce liability?
Answer_EN: Tax reliefs reduce your chargeable income before applying tax rates. E.g., if you earn N2m annually and claim N500k in reliefs, you pay tax on N1.5m only, significantly reducing your total tax bill.
Keywords: exempt, relief, pension, minimum wage, NGO, deduction, N800k
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 162 & Fifth Schedule

Question_EN: Are gifts subject to taxation?
Answer_EN: Gifts are not directly taxable for the recipient under Nigerian tax law. However, the giver may face CGT on asset disposal, and significant or regular gifts may be assessed as income in some circumstances.
Keywords: exempt, relief, pension, minimum wage, NGO, deduction, N800k
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 162 & Fifth Schedule

Question_EN: What benefits are available to taxpayers?
Answer_EN: Benefits available: rent relief (20% of rent, max N500k), pension tax deduction (8% basic), NHF deduction (2.5%), NHIS deduction, life insurance deduction, and 5-year tax-free period for agricultural businesses.
Keywords: exempt, relief, pension, minimum wage, NGO, deduction, N800k
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 162 & Fifth Schedule

Question_EN: How is digital income taxed in Nigeria?
Answer_EN: All digital income is taxable: employment income from remote work via PIT/PAYE; business income from e-commerce/freelancing via PIT (sole trader) or CIT (company); crypto gains via CGT.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: Do content creators need to pay tax?
Answer_EN: Yes. Content creators (YouTubers, bloggers, podcasters, TikTokers) are self-employed. All revenue from AdSense, brand deals, merchandise, and sponsorships must be declared and taxed annually.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: Is income from remote work taxable?
Answer_EN: Yes. Nigerian residents doing remote work pay PIT on their earnings regardless of the employer's location. Foreign employers of Nigerian remote staff do not need to register in Nigeria.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: How do I report earnings from online platforms?
Answer_EN: Gather platform statements showing total earnings. Convert FX amounts to Naira using CBN rates. Include total in your annual PIT return under self-employment/other income. Keep all payment records.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: Are cryptocurrency transactions taxed?
Answer_EN: Yes. Selling, swapping, or using crypto creates taxable events. Gains are taxable under CGT provisions. Losses can only offset crypto gains. Mining income is taxed as business income.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: What tax rules apply to e-commerce businesses?
Answer_EN: E-commerce businesses pay PIT (sole trader) or CIT (company) on profits, charge VAT at 7.5% on taxable goods/services, and comply with WHT rules. Foreign platforms must register under SEP rules.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: Do foreign digital companies pay tax in Nigeria?
Answer_EN: Yes, if they have Significant Economic Presence (SEP) in Nigeria — typically by having annual Nigerian revenue above the prescribed threshold. They must register with FIRS and pay CIT on Nigerian income.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: Are social media earnings taxable?
Answer_EN: Yes. All social media earnings — brand deals, affiliate commissions, platform monetization, sponsored posts — are taxable self-employment income in Nigeria.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: What records should I keep for digital income?
Answer_EN: Keep: platform payment statements (monthly/annual), bank statements showing deposits, invoices for brand deals/contracts, CBN exchange rates used for FX conversion, and expense receipts related to content creation.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)

Question_EN: How can I stay compliant with digital tax laws?
Answer_EN: Register for TIN, file annual PIT return, declare all digital income, convert FX at CBN rates, register for VAT if turnover exceeds N25m, keep all records, and pay assessed tax by March 31st.
Keywords: digital, online, crypto, YouTube, influencer, e-commerce, SEP, 2025
Source: Nigeria Tax Act 2025, Section 9; SEP Order 2020 (amended)
